sec.title: Distance between central venous catheter tips can affect antibiotic clearance during continuous renal replacement therapy
A Bandert1, M Lipcsey2, R Frithiof3, A Larsson4, D Smekal3

1Centre for Research and Development, Uppsala University/Gävleborg, Dept of Surgical Science, Gävle, Sweden, 2Hedenstierna Laboratory, Uppsala University, Dept of Surgical Science, Uppsala, Sweden, 3Dept of Surgical Science, Uppsala University, Dept of Surgical Science, Uppsala, Sweden, 4Dept of Medical Science, Uppsala University, Dept of Medical Science, Uppsala, Sweden
sec.p: Critical Care 2023, 27(S1): P312

Introduction: The aim of this experimental study was to elucidate if different distances between central venous catheter tips can affect drug clearance, during continuous renal replacement therapy (CRRT). Central venous catheters (CVC) are often used in intensive care patients for drug infusion. If a patient receives CRRT, a second central dialysis catheter (CDC) is required. Where to insert CVCs is directed by guidelines but there are no recommendation how to place multiple catheters [1]. If a drug is infused in a CVC with the tip close to the CDC, it could be aspirated into the dialysis machine and by that, increasing the risk of elevated drug clearance [2]. This phenomenon of direct aspiration has been described in vitro, in case studies and in one experimental study [2–4]. If there is a difference in clearance, when CVC and CDC tips are at different positions is less described.

Methods: In this experimental model with 18 pigs, gentamicin (GM) and vancomycin (VM) were infused through a CVC, during CRRT. Clearance was measured when the tip of the CVC was 2 cm caudally of the tip of the CDC, at the same level and at 2 and 4 cm cranially of the tip of the CDC. Position was controlled by chest radiography. Serum and dialysate concentrations were sampled after 20 min of CRRT at each position. Clearance was calculated. Repeated measure ANOVA was performed, level of significance was set to p < 0.05.

Results: When GM and VM were infused through a CVC the distances between the CVC and CDC tips affected clearance of GM (F = 2,91, p = 0.05) and VM (F = 2.98, p = 0.04), during CRRT (see Fig. 1). Clearance was higher when infusion was in a CVC tip cranially to the CDC tip.
Conclusions: The distance between CVC and CDC tips can affect drug clearance, during CRRT.

list-item.p: Frykholm P et al. Acta Anaesthesiol Scand 2014;58:508–24.
Frithiof R et al. ASAIO J 2019;65:408–13.
Stricker KH et al. Anesth Analg 2009;109:1640–3.
Kam KY et al. Anaesthesia 2012;67:115–21.

caption.p: Fig. 1 (abstract P312). Gentamicin (GM) and vancomycin (VM) were infused in a central venous catheter (CVC) during continuous renal replacement therapy (CRRT). The CVC tip was placed in different positions in relation to the central dialysis catheter (CDC), 2 cm caudally (GM 2 and VM 2), at the same tip level (GM 0 and VM 0), 2 cm cranially (GM-2 and VM-2) and 4 cm cranially (GM-4 and VM-4). Clearance was measured at the different positions

sec.title: Comparative analysis between mixed venous-to-arterial CO2 gap and lactate values during liver transplantation
D Rodriguez Villar, B Villa Cigales, P Gómez Aguilar, L Martínez Botet, J Villahoz, L Gajate
Hospital Universitario Ramón y Cajal, Anesthesiology, Madrid, Spain
sec.p: Critical Care 2023, 27(S1): P313

Introduction: Patients going through liver transplantation suffer hemodynamic and metabolic changes. Ensuring adequate tissue perfusion is essential in this situation. Mixed venous-to-arterial CO2 tension gap [p(v−a)CO2] defined as the difference between mixed venous blood pCO2 and arterial pCO2 has been used as a sensitive marker of the microcirculatory state in patients with septic shock. This study analises the correlation between p(v−a)CO2 and lactate values during liver transplantation.

Methods: A correlation analysis between lactate and p(v−a)CO2 was conducted at the different stages of liver transplantation at Ramon y Cajal Hospital. The Shapiro–Wilk normality test was used to rule out normal distribution. Spearman’s correlation between lactate and p(v−a)CO2 was assessed. p value < 0.05 was defined as statistically significant.

Results: Perioperative data of 98 patients were collected. Lactate median at baseline was 1.2 mmol/l (IQR 0.6) and p(v−a)CO2 median 4 mmHg (IQR 5). Spearman’s correlation coefficient was r = 0.25 (p = 0.03). Lactate median in anhepatic phase was 4 mmol/l (IQR 1,1) and p(v−a)CO2 median 4 mm Hg (IQR 4.8). Spearman’s correlation coefficient was r = −0.1 (p = 0.2). Lactate median in neohepatic phase was 3.1 mmol/l (IQR 1.3) and p(v−a)CO2 median 3 mmHg (IQR 5). Spearman’s correlation coefficient was r = −0.1 (p = 0.07). Lactate median at ICU admision was 2.25 mmol/l (IQR 2.2) and p(v−a)CO2 median 4 mmHg (IQR 4). Spearman’s correlation coefficient was r = −0.01 (p = 0.9). Lactate median 24 h after liver transplantation was 1.2 mmol/l (IQR 1.1) and p(v−a)CO2 median 4 mmHg (IQR 3). Spearman’s correlation coefficient was r = −0.1 (p = 0.4).

Conclusions: We only found correlation between lactate and p(v−a)CO2 in baseline measurements before surgery. There was no correlation during the other phases of liver transplantation which leads us to think maybe p(v−a)CO2 could be a better indicator of microcirculatory state and tisular perfusion in these patients, since lactate clearance is dependent on liver function.

sec.title: Gastroesophageal reflux disease and its health-related quality of life among adult ICU survivors
S Wali, M Gurjar, S Gutte, S Mohindra, B Poddar, A Azim, V Kapoor, P Mishra
Sanjay Gandhi Postgraduate Institute of Medical Sciences, Critical Care Medicine, LUCKNOW, India
sec.p: Critical Care 2023, 27(S1): P314
Introduction: We aimed to study gastroesophageal reflux disease (GERD) prevalence, its health-related quality of life (GERD-HRQL) and risk factors for new-onset GERD in ICU survivors.

Methods: After ethics approval, this prospective study done at 20-bed ICU of a university hospital (Trial Identifier NCT05193266). GERD is defined as presence of heartburn and/or regurgitation symptom ≥ 2 times a week (World Gastroenterology Organization-2017). All adult survivors screened at ICU discharge. Exclusion criteria: ICU stay < 96 h, presence of GCS < 15, feeding or tracheostomy tube, abdominal drain/surgery and pregnancy. Patients having GERD assessed for GERD-HRQL score and its changes over 6 weeks follow-up.

Results: During study period (Jan-Aug 2022), 151 admitted and 105 survived. After exclusion of 34 patients, 71 included with age 42 (28–58) years, 60% male, SOFA score 7 (4–11) and Charlson comorbidity index 0 (0–3). ICU stay was 15 (6–31) days, 59% needs ventilator (MV), 45% vasopressor; 14% dialysis (RRT). Feeding tube (NG) present in 76% (15 days) and tracheal tube (TT) in 46% (18 days). Sedation (49%), steroid (37%), prokinetic (39%) and other at-risk drug used in 62%. 23 patients (32%) had GERD prior to current illness (Fig. 1a). In remaining 48 patients, 17 (24%) had new-onset GERD with OR [95% CI] for risk factors: age 0.95 [0.9–0.9] (p = 0.02), comorbidity 0.92 [0.7–1.2], SOFA score 0.92 [0.7–1.2], GI disease 0.97 [0.3–3.2], ICU stay 1.0 [0.9–1.0], MV 1.33 [0.4–4.4], vasopressor 1.2 [0.3–4.2], RRT 0.69 [0.1–4.0], NG days 1.0 [0.9–1], TT days 0.99 [0.9–1.0], sedation 1.78 [0.5–5.9], steroid 0.66 [0.1–2.3], prokinetic 0.66 [0.1–2.3] and at-risk drugs 0.3 [0–1]. In follow-up, GERD-HRQL score improved significantly in new onset GERD group from 9 (6–14) to 4 (3–9) (p = 0.002); while didn’t in pre-illness GERD group from 3 (0–7) to 3 (0–8) (p = 0.51) (Fig. 1b, c).

Conclusions: One-fourth ICU survivors had new onset GERD; and its HRQL score improved significantly at 6-week follow-up.
Fig. 1 (abstract P314). a Prevalence of GERD among adult ICU survivors; b, c Change in GERD-HRQL score over 6 week follow-up

sec.title: Mortality, management and risk factors of non-occlusive mesenteric ischemia: evidence from systematic review and population-wide study
V Mihnovitš1, AL Voomets2, K Kase3, J Starkopf2, A Reintam Blaser4, K Tamme2

1Tartu University Hospital, Intensive Care, Tartu, Estonia, 2Tartu University, Institute of Clinical Medicine, Tartu, Estonia, 3Tartu University Hospital, Surgery Clinic, Tartu, Estonia, 4Lucerne Cantonal Hospital, Center of Intensive Care Medicine, Lucerne, Switzerland
sec.p: Critical Care 2023, 27(S1): P315
Introduction: The study aim was to characterize the demography, management and mortality of patients with non-occlusive mesenteric ischemia (NOMI).
Methods: We performed a substudy on NOMI based on two recent studies [1, 2]. Studies reporting data about NOMI were reviewed and data extracted using a predeveloped form.

Results: We reviewed 23 studies with a total of 2865 NOMI patients. Management and mortality are depicted in Table 1. Ten studies with 370 patients reported an average age of 68.8 years (min 37 and max 98). 46.4% of the patients were female in 14 studies (2691 patients) reporting gender. In seven studies (163 patients) reporting localisation of ischemia the small bowel, large bowel and combination of both was described in 30.6%, 31.4% and 38% of cases, respectively. Main risk factors were heart failure (mean 70.5% in four studies in 302 patients), vasopressor use (mean 40.2% in four studies in 1032 patients) and prior surgery (mean 21% in five studies in 114 patients).

Conclusions: NOMI seems to affect younger population compared to other forms of acute mesenteric ischemia. Frequently both small and large intestine are involved. Low cardiac output and use of vasopressors are important contributing factors. Overall mortality is high but seems to be lower in the vasodilator therapy group.

list-item.p: Tamme K et al. BMJ Open. 2022;12:e062846.
Kase K et al. World J Surg. 2023;47:173–181.

caption.p: Table 1 (abstract P315). The management and mortality of patients with non-occlusive mesenteric ischemia reported in different studies

sec.title: Deep neural network models for detection of strangulating bowel obstruction using contrast enhanced CT
N Okada, M Yoshida, R Noda, D Inoue, N Uozumi, H Matsuki, H Shintani, N Kataoka, T Yamaguchi
Kishiwada Tokusyukai Hospital, Division of Surgeon, Osaka, Japan
sec.p: Critical Care 2023, 27(S1): P316

Introduction: Strangulating bowel obstruction should be treated immediately. Contrast enhanced CT is widely accepted to classify bowel obstructions. However, the diagnostic imaging criteria are not clearly defined. Accuracy of diagnostic imaging varies with physicians and even specialist can overlook strangulating bowel obstructions. We use AI models to standardize diagnostic imaging and improve clinical outcome of these disease. In this study, we aimed to develop and validate deep neural network (DNN) models detecting strangulating bowel obstruction using contrast enhanced CT.

Methods: We retrospectively collected 31,100 anonymous contrast enhanced CT images together with their clinical reports at the Kishiwada Tokusyukai Hospital between January 1, 2020, and December 31, 2022. We excluded incomplete, younger than 7 years old, and poorly depicted images. The part of this dataset from August 1, 2022, until December 31, 2022, was used for external test and the rest for training and validation. We develop and evaluate two binary-classification DNN models: slice model examining a CT slice to find lesions; series model examining a series of CT images to find a suspected patient. Accuracy, and Areas under the receiver operating characteristic curves (AUCs) were used to assess the models.

Results: The training and validation dataset contained 25,860 images. The test dataset contained 5240 images. The series model achieved 80% accuracy and an AUC of 0.72 for detecting strangulating bowel obstruction, the slice model achieved 72% accuracy and AUC of 0.62.

Conclusions: Our results show that DNN models can potentially identify strangulating bowel obstruction using contrast enhanced CT. Increasing the number of samples, DNN model could accurately detect strangulating bowel obstruction.

sec.title: Safety and feasibility of kefir in the critically ill
SSR Rajendraprasad1, DR Ramachandran2, SZ Zec3, JS Skalski1, JB Bakken4, WDF Freeman5, SRA Ahmad1, ASS Shah6, SK Khanna7, LVK Karnatovskaia1

1Mayo Clinic, Pulmonary and Critical Care, Rochester, USA, 2Mayo Clinic, Hematology, Rochester, USA, 3Beth Israel Deaconess Medical Center, Anesthesia, Critical Care and Pain Medicine, Boston, USA, 4St Luke’s Hospital, Infectious Diseases, Duluth, USA, 5Mayo Clinic, Neurosurgery, Jacksonville, USA, 6Mayo Clinic, Infectious Diseases, Rochester, USA, 7Mayo Clinic, Gastroenterology, Rochester, USA
sec.p: Critical Care 2023, 27(S1): P317

Introduction: Dysbiosis occurs from disruption to the microbiome, resulting in the expansion of pathogenic strains and a loss of bacterial diversity in the gastrointestinal tract. Within hours of critical illness, 90% of commensal organisms are lost, increasing morbidity and mortality. Probiotic supplementation failed to ameliorate dysbiosis in this population. Fermented foods have been shown to increase microbiome diversity and decrease inflammatory markers. This study investigated the safety and feasibility of administering kefir (a fermented milk beverage containing multiple cultures of commensal bacteria) as the first step to using fermented foods to mitigate dysbiosis in the critically ill.

Methods: We conducted an open-label phase I safety and feasibility investigation of kefir given in escalating doses to adult critically ill patients with intact gastrointestinal tracts, starting with 60 ml, then 120 ml 12 h later, followed by 240 ml daily. Gastrointestinal symptoms were monitored daily while patients received kefir in the medical intensive care unit.

Results: 52 patients participated (mean age 64 years (range 23–88), 62% male). No serious adverse events were observed other than diarrhea in two patients who also had documented laxative use. The median Acute Physiology and Chronic Health Evaluation II score was 35.5 (IQR 23.5–56.9). Kefir was administered via gastric tube to 28 patients, and the rest took it orally. Of the 370 doses ordered, 336 were administered (91%) and met our feasibility target (NPO status was the most common reason a dose was not given). There was no evidence of bacteremia related to kefir. Two patients discontinued study participation due to distaste and bloating.
Conclusions: Administration of kefir to critically ill adults appears to be safe and feasible. A larger evaluation of its safety, tolerability, and impact on dysbiosis and outcomes is warranted.

sec.title: Gut microbiome as a risk factor for mortality among sepsis patients of extreme severity
E Karakike1, G Damoraki2, E Stylianakis2, P Koufargyris2, I Kollias2, EJ Giamarellos-Bourboulis2

12nd Department of Critical Care, National and Kapodistrian University of Athens, 2nd Department of Critical Care, Athens, Greece, 24th Department of Internal Medicine, National and Kapodistrian University of Athens, 4th Department of Internal Medicine, Athens, Greece
sec.p: Critical Care 2023, 27(S1): P318

Introduction: Gut dysbiosis is frequently seen at sepsis onset, but impact on sepsis outcome remains unclear [1]. We aimed to assess microbiome diversity and composition, with regards to 28-day mortality and secondary infections in sepsis.

Methods: This is a secondary analysis of the INCLASS randomized trial (NCT03345992), evaluating the efficacy of clarithromycin treatment in patients with sepsis-associated respiratory distress and other organ dysfunctions [2]. Rectal microbiome was assessed for all patients on day 1, after DNA extraction and 16S gene—based sequencing on Oxford Nanopore MinION followed by EPI2ME analysis (ONT/Metrichore Ltd). All two-tailed, Bonferroni-corrected, p values < 0.05 were considered significant.

Results: After quality assessment, 102 samples were included in the analysis. Biodiversity, assessed by Shannon and Simpson indexes, was significantly reduced among patients who died (p = 0.003 and p = 0.005), but greater among those with sepsis resolution (v = 0.010 and v = 0.018, respectively). The Shannon Index was an independent risk factor for mortality [OR 0.497; 95% Confidence intervals (CI) 0.286–0.864; p = 0.013], after adjustment for sequential organ failure assessment (SOFA) score, Charlson comorbidity index, and type of initial infection. Survivors displayed greater abundance in Actinobacteria (p = 0.00205) and Bacteroidetes (p = 0.01745) than non-survivors; distribution of Proteobacteria (p = 0.39299) and Firmicutes (p = 0.29353) did not differ (Fig. 1). Higher abundance in Actinobacteria was less likely associated with primary bacteremia during follow-up (p = 0.03264).

Conclusions: Gut dysbiosis is associated with 28-day mortality in sepsis, independently of severity scores. Higher abundance in Actinobacteria may be protective, possibly due to decreased bacterial translocation related to overgrowth of other phyla. Targeted interventions may improve sepsis outcome.

list-item.p: Adelman MW et al. Crit Care. 2020;24:278.
Karakike E et al. Crit Care. 2022;26:183.

caption.p: Fig. 1 (abstract P318). Differences in baseline gut microbiome composition for sepsis survivors and non-survivors by day 28. *Depicts significance of p values after correction for comparisons in relative abundance

sec.title: The gut microbiota composition has no predictive value for the endotoxin-induced immune response or development of endotoxin tolerance in humans in vivo
N Bruse1, A Jansen1, J Gerretsen1, D Rijbroek1, K Wienholts2, M Arron3, H Van Goor3, T Ederveen4, P Pickkers1, M Kox1

1Radboudumc, Department of Intensive Care Medicine, Nijmegen, Netherlands, 2Amsterdam UMC location University of Amsterdam, Surgery, Amsterdam, Netherlands, 3Radboudumc, Department of Surgery, Nijmegen, Netherlands, 4Radboudumc, Center for Molecular and Biomolecular Informatics (CMBI), Nijmegen, Netherlands
sec.p: Critical Care 2023, 27(S1): P319

Introduction: Murine studies indicate that the gut microbiome may influence immunity. However, it is largely unknown whether this also applies to systemic immune responses, especially in humans. If so, profiling the gut microbiota may aid precision medicine approaches in critically ill patients.

Methods: The fecal microbiota composition of 108 healthy participants was determined using 16S rRNA marker gene sequencing. Immunological phenotypes were determined using (1) an ex vivo challenge model in which monocytes were stimulated with various inflammatory compounds, and (2) an in vivo challenge model of systemic inflammation and immunological tolerance induced by repeated intravenous administration of 1 ng/kg body weight E. coli endotoxin. This model captures hallmarks of both the hyperinflammatory and immunosuppressive phenotypes of sepsis.

Results: Redundancy analysis (RDA) revealed that the gut microbiota composition was associated with sex, but not with age, BMI, or diet (Fig. 1a). Significant associations were observed between the microbiota composition and ex vivo monocytic cytokine responses induced by several stimuli, although the explained variance was relatively low (0.3–4.8%, Fig. 1b). A number of pairwise correlations between Blautia, Bacteroides and Prevotella genera and cytokine production induced by these stimuli were identified (Fig. 1c). No significant relationships between microbiota composition and in vivo cytokine responses or tolerance were found (explained variance ranging from 0.4 to 1.3%, Fig. 1d,e).

Conclusions: Although the gut microbiota composition does explain some of the variance in ex vivo cytokine responses, its predictive value is limited. Furthermore, the gut microbiota composition is neither associated with in vivo systemic immune responses to endotoxin in humans, nor with immunological tolerance. Therefore, our findings do not support considering the gut microbiota composition for precision medicine approaches targeting the immune system in critically ill patients.
Fig. 1 (abstract P319). A Explained variance calculated using redundancy analysis (RDA) for non-immune-related covariates. B Explained variance calculated using redundancy analysis (RDA) for ex vivo stimulus—cytokine pairs for all participants. C Pairwise correlations between the 25 most abundant microbiota on genus level and the ex vivo stimulus—cytokine pairs for all participants. D Explained variance calculated using redundancy analysis (RDA) for in vivo cytokine responses upon the first LPS challenge (day 0)

sec.title: Association between gastrointestinal tract function and mortality in critically ill patients with suspected sepsis
M Roimi1, A Shrot2, R Ilan1, D Epstein1, Y Bar-Lavie1
1Rambam Health-Care Campus, Critical Care Division, Haifa,, Israel, 2Rambam Health-Care Campus, Independent researcher, Haifa, Israel
sec.p: Critical Care 2023, 27(S1): P320

Introduction: Evaluating a patient suspected of having sepsis mandates an assessment of organ dysfunction. Current severity scores do not assess gastrointestinal (GI) tract function. We aimed to determine the association between GI tract function on the days preceding presumed infection with the clinical outcome.

Methods: A retrospective cohort study including all the adult patients admitted to the 18 beds general ICU in an academic tertiary medical center between January 2013 and December 2020. We included patients with suspected infection after at least 72-h from ICU admission. Patients with a colostomy, ileostomy, or a rectal tube were excluded. The main exposures were the number of bowel movements (BM) and consecutive days without BMs during the preceding 72-h. The primary outcome was 30-day all-cause mortality. Multivariate logistic regression was performed to assess for possible confounders including factors associate with GI tract motility, antibiotics, length of stay, APACHE II, and SOFA scores.

Results: The cohort included 1306 patients. The median age was 56.2 years (IQR 39.469.1); 1004 (70.0%) were male. At the time of suspected infection, 1139 (79.4%) were treated with antibiotics, and the median SOFA score was 5.0 (IQR 3.0–7.0). The 30-day all-cause mortality rate was 364 (25.4%). Patients who had 0–2, 3–5, and > 5 BMs during the 72-h preceding the suspected infection had mortality rates of 20.3%, 28.0%, and 34.3% (p < 0.001) (Fig. 1). Patients who had > 2 BMs had an adjusted OR of 1.55 (95% CI 1.13–2.1, p = 0.005) for mortality. Every additional consecutive day without BM before the suspected infectious event was associated with an adjusted OR of 0.84 (95% CI 0.73–0.95, p < 0.02) for mortality.

Conclusions: In patients suspected to have sepsis, a higher frequency of BMs during the preceding 72-h, is associated with increased mortality. Further studies are needed to confirm our findings and investigate the physiologic mechanisms related to the GI tract function during sepsis.
Fig. 1 (abstract P320). The association between gastrointestinal tract function during the 72-h preceding suspected infection and 30-day all-cause mortality

sec.title: The accuracy of pH strip test and pH liquid test comparing with the standard values from pH meter in measuring gastric pH from medical critically ill patients
K Phukpattanachai1, N Praditseree1, S Skjolaas1, S Klaychaiya1, K Trongtrakul2
1Faculty of Medicine Chiang Mai University, Chiang Mai, Thailand, 2Faculty of Medicine Chiang Mai University, Department of Internal Medicine, Chiang Mai, Thailand
sec.p: Critical Care 2023, 27(S1): P321

Introduction: Gastric pH measurement can be utilized to confirm the position of nasogastric (NG) tube insertion. Thus, our study aimed to determine the accuracy of more convenient methods by the pH strip test (pHS) and pH liquid test (pHL), compared to the standard pH meter (pHM) in measuring gastric pH values in critically ill patients.

Methods: A prospective observational study was conducted on patients who were admitted to medical ICUs of the Faculty of Medicine, Chiang Mai University (MedCMU), Chiang Mai, Thailand, between June and July 2022. Gastric samples were measured by pHM, pHS, and pHL techniques and interpreted by four independent raters. The reliability of pHS and pHL readings reported by intraclass correlation coefficients (ICC) were 0.93 (0.90, 0.96) and 0.94 (0.90, 0.96), respectively.

Results: A total of 113 gastric samples were collected from 27 ICU patients, which were 71 (IQR 56, 81) year-old, male 48% (13/27 cases), sepsis/septic shock 67% (18/27 cases), SOFA score 5 (IQR 2.9), and serum lactate 3.7 (IQR 2.2, 5.6) mmol/l. The pH values measured from pHM, pHS, and pHL were 5.83 (IQR 5.12, 6.61), 5.50 (IQR 4.88, 6.00), and 5.75 (IQR 5.25, 6.38), respectively, p < 0.01. The pH value from the pHS method was slightly lower than the pHM, with a difference of −0.25 (−0.54, 0.03), p < 0.001. While the pH value from pHL was slightly higher than the pHM, with a difference of 0.02 (−0.36, 0.53), p = 0.04. The accuracy of pH measurement from pHS had a good correlation with pHM, with Spearman’s correlation (rho) of 0.91 (0.86 to 0.96) and the linear regression equation Y = 0.95 X + 0.56 (Fig. 1A (abstract P321)). On the other hand, there was an optimal correlation between pHL and pHM, with rho = 0.75 (0.60 –0.89) and the equation Y = 1.09 X −0.72 (Fig. 1B).

Conclusions: The use of the pHS has a better correlation with the standard pHM measurement than the pHL. The utilized pHS in measuring gastric pH might be an option to ensure the tip of the NG tube position in critically ill patients.
Fig. 1 (abstract P321). The Spearman’s correlation and linear regression equation of (A) pH strip test vs. pH meter and (B) pH liquid test versus pH meter

sec.title: Observational study: the risk of malnutrition in post-ICU COVID-19 patients in Dubai, UAE
W Ayesh1, F Dhinda2, S Noufal2, N Murad2
1Al Tadawi General Hospital and Medical Center, Dubai, United Arab Emirates, 2Dubai Health Authority (DHA), Clinical Nutrition Department, Dubai, United Arab Emirates
sec.p: Critical Care 2023, 27(S1): P322

Introduction: Due to the impact of the severe acute respiratory syndrome coronavirus 2 (SARS-CoV-2) on the humoral immune system, gastrointestinal, and metabolic activities, malnutrition in COVID-19 is inevitable. This study aimed to assess the prevalence, identify COVID-19 patients at risk of malnutrition, and determine the nutritional risk profile of COVID-19 patients and the need for ongoing nutritional support after ICU stay.

Methods: A monocentric observational study based on data collected from 200 COVID-19 patients at hospital discharge in Dubai, UAE. Male and female residents and citizens (≥ 18 years) who tested positive for COVID-19 upon ICU admission and who were ready for discharge were included. The ‘MUST’ malnutrition screening was performed to identify patients at high risk of malnutrition who required ONS and other treatments.

Results: The present study included two hundred patients where male participants constituted 68% compared to females (32%). The included population was neither acutely ill nor had nutritional intake for more than 5 days. 45% of COVID-19 patients experienced a reduced dietary intake at hospital, and 58% lost weight during ICU/hospital stays. About 25% received enteral nutrition in the ICU, whereas (2%) required ongoing homecare nutritional support after hospital discharge. Almost 80% were advised to follow up with a dietitian and 96% were provided additional dietary counseling. Regarding the COVID-19 patients' post-ICU stay nutritional support, the adjusted odds ratio of follow-up consultation with dietitian significantly decreased by 66% among patients aged from 18 to 49 years, compared to older patients (ORa = 0.34, 95% CI 0.12–0.86, p = 0.032).

Conclusions: Close assessment, evaluation, and monitoring of malnutrition are critical in severely ill COVID-19 patients post-ICU. ONS is highly recommended for high-risk patients to provide support against muscle loss during ICU stay and improve the recovery of the patients at discharge.

sec.title: Ultrasound assessment of muscle mass loss in the burned patient and its association with prognosis
RI Prado1, MT Tanita2, LTQ Cardoso3, CMC Grion3

1University Hospital – Londrina State University, Critical Care, Londrina, Brazil, 2University Hospital – Londrina State University, Intensivist physician, Londrina, Brazil, 3University Hospital – Londrina State University, Department of Internal Medicine, Londrina State University, Londrina, Brazil
sec.p: Critical Care 2023, 27(S1): P323

Introduction: Loss of muscle mass may be a predictor of mortality in major burn patients, since patients who develop skeletal muscle tissue degradation have longer hospital stays, prolonged mechanical ventilation. The mortality rate in patients with severe burns is between 1.4 and 18% [1]. The aim of this study was to evaluate the loss of lean mass in burn patients through ultrasound of the quadriceps muscle of the thigh in burn patients admitted to a specialized bed at a university hospital and to correlate the results with the prognosis.

Methods: Prospective longitudinal study performed using ultrasound of the quadriceps muscle of the thigh to assess the change in thickness in millimeters on day 1, day 3, and day 7 after study enrollment, in 45 burn patients admitted to a burn treatment center (BTC) of a University Hospital, between April 2020 and September 2021. Patients with burns on the thighs, which prevented the performance of the exams, were excluded.

Results: Correlation was observed between the variation in muscle thickness in the interval from Day 1 to Day 3 and age (p = 0.035). Loss of muscle thickness was observed in all patients between Day 1 and Day 7. The median thickness for all patients on Day 1 was 24.50 mm (ITQ 21.22–30.85) and on Day 7 it was 18.80 (ITQ 16.07–23.62), with p = 0.0001. The median quadriceps muscle thickness of the patients admitted to the ward on Day 1 was 27.35 mm (ITQ 21.00–35.00) and on Day 7 it was 22.35 mm (ITQ 15.70–27.90), with p = 0.038. The median thickness of patients admitted to the ICU on Day 1 was 23.40 mm (ITQ 21.30–27.00) and on Day 7 it was 18.00 mm (ITQ 16.67–22.02), with p = 0.0005.

Conclusions: Muscle loss occurred early and rapidly within the first seven days of hospitalization. An association was observed between loss of muscle thickness and age, but no association was observed with the extent of the burn, hospital stay, occurrence of healthcare-associated infection, or mortality.

list-item.p: Brusselaers N et al. Crit Care 2010;14:R188

sec.title: The role of sarcopenia in the mortality of critically ill patients in the ICU
F Moniati1, C Chatzimatthaiou2
1Barts and The London, School of Medicine, London, UK, 2Barts and The London, London, UK
sec.p: Critical Care 2023, 27(S1): P324

Introduction: First proposed by Dr. Rosenberg, sarcopenia, the loss of skeletal muscle mass, involves a decline in clinical performance, increased mortality and morbidity. In the critically ill patients in ICU, mortality is still a challenge, particularly for elderly patients with functional decline. The relationship between mortality in ICU critically ill patients and sarcopenia is inconsistent throughout literature, impeding our ability to quantify the magnitude of the problem. Our primary objective is to evaluate the role of sarcopenia in the mortality of the critically ill patients in ICU through a systematic review.

Methods: This systematic review was conducted using the Cochrane, Embase and PubMed databases to examine the studies available for the association between sarcopenia and mortality in the critically ill patients.

Results: Overall, eleven retrospective cohort studies totalling 2029 patients, all aged 65 years of age and older, were included in our review with the primary outcome of all studies being mortality. As far as the methods of skeletal muscle measurement using Computerized Tomography (CT) are concerned, six studies used skeletal muscle index (SMI) (n = 832), three studies used skeletal muscle area (SMA) (n = 711) and two studies used total psoas muscle area (TPA) (n = 486). The use of SMI demonstrated an increased risk of mortality in critically ill patients with sarcopenia with 95% confidence interval (CI) = 1.75–2.82. Similar results were also shown using SMA (CI = 2.73–4.47) or TPA (CI = 1.85–6.30).

Conclusions: Evidently, these results illustrate that critically ill patients in ICU with sarcopenia hold a greater risk of mortality compared with patients without the condition. Effective intervention programmes such as early mobilization and high protein diet can help reduce the mortality risk and provide beneficial outcomes.

sec.title: The association of sarcopenia defined by a simpler screening tool and long-term outcomes
T Yuyen, C Thanakiattiwibun, O Chaiwat
Department of Anesthesiology, Faculty of Medicine, Siriraj Hospital, Mahidol University, Department of Anesthesiology, Bangkok, Thailand
sec.p: Critical Care 2023, 27(S1): P325

Introduction: Sarcopenia and frailty are common in older adult patients and associated with poor postoperative outcomes. Low muscle mass and function are main indicators of sarcopenia. The simpler screening tool without muscle mass measurement, C3 formula, demonstrated high predictive power for diagnosing sarcopenia [1]. The aim of this study was to identify the association of sarcopenia defined by C3 formula and long-term outcomes in older adult cancer patients undergoing surgery.

Methods: Cancerous surgical patients aged older than 60 years were enrolled. The sarcopenia was defined by C3 formula, consisted of low muscle strength and/or abnormal physical performance, plus malnutrition/risk of malnutrition. The muscle strength was measured by handgrip-strength. The physical performance was assessed by the 6-m usual gait speed walk test. Nutritional status was evaluated by the Mini Nutritional Assessment–Short Form (MNA-SF). After sarcopenia screening, the long-term outcomes were collected including the Barthel Index of Activities of Daily Living (ADL) scores, any infections in the hospital, length of hospital stay, and mortality at 3 months and 1 year.

Results: 251 patients were enrolled. 130 patients were sarcopenic defined by C3 formula. Sarcopenic patients had greater number of moderate to severe disability (ADL score ≤ 70) at 3 months after hospital discharge (19.5% vs 5.2%, P = 0.001) and higher mortality rate at 1 year after sarcopenia screening (29.5% vs 14.9%, P = 0.006). There were no differences regarding the infection rate, length of hospital stay and in-hospital mortality rate. The independent risk factors for one year mortality were distant organ metastasis (HR: 3.99, 95% CI 2.25–7.07) and sarcopenia defined by C3 formula (HR: 1.78, 95% CI 1.01–3.15).

Conclusions: Sarcopenia defined by a simpler screening tool was associated with higher moderate to severe disability at 3 months after discharge and increased mortality rate at 1 year after screening.

list-item.p: Chaiwat O et al. PLoS One. 2021;16:e0257672

sec.title: Effectiveness and safety of early enteral feeding in critically ill patients with sepsis: a systematic review and meta-analysis
RE Ko1, SJ Moon2, CM Park1, GY Suh1, CR Chung1
1Samsung Medical Center, Critical Care Medicine, Seoul, South Korea, 2Samsung Medical Center, Medicine, Seoul, South Korea
sec.p: Critical Care 2023, 27(S1): P326

Introduction: Although early enteral nutrition (EN) may prevent muscle catabolism, it is concerning in sepsis because of increased risks of intestinal ischemia. We aimed to redefine the evidence regarding the effectiveness and timing of early EN using a systematic review that focused on critically ill patients with sepsis.

Methods: We searched MEDLINE, Embase, Cumulative Index to Nursing and Allied Health Literature, Cochrane Central Register of Controlled Trials, ClinicalTrials.gov, and ICTRP databases on February 23, 2022. Our systematic review included studies published after 2004 that compared outcomes of early EN with those of delayed EN in critically ill patients with sepsis aged ≥ 18 years. Two authors independently reviewed the title, abstract, and full text and selected relevant studies according to the inclusion criteria. Two authors independently extracted patient information and reports on effectiveness, adverse effects, and clinical outcomes.

Results: Ten studies were included, and the sample size was 31–2410. The pooled risk ratio of mortality obtained using three randomized controlled trials was 1.02 (95% confidence interval [CI] 0.85–1.24; I2 = 3.15%, p = 0.26), which was not significant. The pooled mean difference of length of stay in the intensive care unit obtained using two randomized controlled trials was -1.52 days (95% CI − 3.18 to 0.15; I2 = 47.62%, p = 0.17), which was not significant. The pooled risk ratio of intestinal ischemia obtained using two randomized controlled trials was 3.53 (95% CI 1.36–9.15; I2 = 0.00%, p = 0.53); this was significantly predominant for early EN. Other outcome measurement indices and adverse effect reports had mixed results.

Conclusions: Early EN intervention did not significantly improve intensive care unit-related mortality but increased intestinal ischemia risks in critically ill patients with sepsis. Heterogeneous characteristics and outcome measurements should be improved for a tailored approach to early EN in critically ill patients with sepsis.

sec.title: Early initiation of fluid intake (sipping) after extubation versus current recommendation, effect of feeling of thirst
V Nigrovičová, Š Sedláčková, M Durila
University Hospital in Motol, Department of Anaesthesiology, Resuscitation and Intensive Care medicine, 2nd Medical Faculty, Charles University, Prague 5, Czech Republic
sec.p: Critical Care 2023, 27(S1): P327

Introduction: Safe extubation is only possible if the patient is conscious and cooperative, able to cough, swallow and has preserved protective reflexes of the respiratory and swallowing tract. Despite that, according to the current recommendation, after extubation they do not receive any liquid orally for 2 h and therefore they feel thirsty. However, there is a lack of evidence in the literature about the validity of this recommendation. The aim of study was to investigate the incidence of thirst and occurrence of complications between patients receiving sipping immediately after extubation and patients who did not drink any fluid within 2 h after extubation.

Methods: In randomized study we assigned 40 patients after short intubation and who were extubated within 48 h. We defined 2 groups with 20 patients in every group. 1st group—early initiation of oral fluid intake in a form of sipping within 2 h, with maximal amount of water 100 ml per hour. 2nd group—oral fluid intake postponed up to 2 h after extubation. Exclusion criteria: patients after upper digestive tract surgery, trachea surgery and with possible neurological deficiency. At defined time points (0, 60 and 120 min after extubation) we evaluated thirst and occurrence of complications (nausea, vomiting, aspiration) between both groups.

Results: Despite the fact we did not find a significant difference in the feeling of thirst at time points—p > 0.05 (probably because of low number of patients, but tendency is obvious), we found, that in the 1st group where patients received sipping, we saw a significant thirst relief in every time point—0, 60, 120 min after extubation—p = 0.0009, 0.0088 and 0.0003. There were no differences in occurrence of complications between both groups.

Conclusions: The study shows that early initiation of fluid intake after extubation, in a form of sipping, seems to be beneficial and safe for patients and decreases incidence of thirst after extubation without any complications.

sec.title: Amino acids effect on muscle and gut functionality in critically ill patients: a proof-of-concept randomized controlled trial
R Bounab1, N Heming1, R Carlier2, H Prigent3, V Maxime1, B Guenoud4, M Hartweg4, C Roessle4, D Breuille4, D Annane1

1Raymond Poincaré hospital, critical care unit, Garches, France, 2 Raymond Poincaré hospital, Medical Imaging, Garches, France, 3Raymond Poincaré hospital, Department of Physiology, Garches, France, 4Société de Produits Nestlé, Nestlé Health Science, Lausanne, Switzerland
sec.p: Critical Care 2023, 27(S1): P328

Introduction: A fraction of critically ill patients require prolonged intensive care unit (ICU) stays [1]. This population is at greater risk of muscle mass and muscle strength loss. Supplementation using a tailored mix of amino acids may strengthen the innate gut defence, promote intestinal mucosa repair and limit muscle loss [2].

Methods: We performed a single-centre, double-blind randomized placebo-controlled study testing a specific combination of five amino acids or placebo mixed with enteral feeding for up to 21 days. Markers of renal function, gut barrier structure and muscle functionality were collected at baseline and 1, 2, 3 and 8 weeks after randomization. Muscle structure and function were assessed by means of MRI measurements of the anterior quadriceps volume and by twitch airway pressure. Data were compared between groups relative to the baseline.

Results: We randomized 35 critically ill patients with sepsis or ARDS. The amino acid blend did not impair urine output, blood creatinine levels or creatinine clearance. Plasma citrulline levels increased significantly during the treatment period in the amino acid group (difference in means [95% CI] 5.86 [1.72; 10.00] nmol/ml, p = 0.007). Alanine aminotransferase and alkaline phosphatase concentrations were lower in the amino acid group than in the placebo group at one week (ratio of means 0.5 [0.29; 0.86] (p = 0.015) and 0.73 [0.57; 0.94] (p = 0.015), respectively). Twitch airway pressure and volume of the anterior quadriceps were greater in the amino acid group than in the placebo group 3 weeks after randomization (difference in means 10.6 [0.99; 20.20] cmH20 (p = 0.035) and 3.12 [0.5; 5.73] cm3/kg (p = 0.022), respectively) (Fig. 1).

Conclusions: The5-amino acid blend supplementation increased plasma citrulline levels, reduced alanine aminotransferase and alkaline phosphatase levels, and improved twitch airway pressure and anterior quadriceps volume.

list-item.p: Nelson JE et al. Am J Respir Crit Care Med 2010;182:446–54.
Plank LD et al. Ann Surg 1998;228:146–58.

caption.p: Fig. 1 (abstract P328). Indexed muscle volume of the anterior compartment of the quadriceps (cc/kg) in the amino acid and placebo groups. Anterior quadriceps volume was measured semi-automatically using isometric T1-weighted 3D images

sec.title: The impact of proning on nutritional therapy in mechanically ventilated COVID-19 patients
N Steinfort, K Dams, R Jacobs, P Jorens
Antwerp University Hospital, ICU, Edegem, Belgium
sec.p: Critical Care 2023, 27(S1): P329

Introduction: Critically ill COVID-19-patients are at high risk of developing ICU-related malnutrition. This study aimed to examine the impact of proning on providing nutritional therapy for mechanically ventilated COVID-19-patients by comparing the achievement of nutritional goals and possible complications in patients who were proned vs. those who were not.

Methods: This is a single-center retrospective cohort study. We included all adult COVID-19 patients admitted to the ICU from 01/03/2020 until 31/05/2020 who required invasive mechanical ventilation (IMV), excluding those referred for ECMO. Data were extracted from electronic patient files. Weight-based nutrition targets were set in agreement with ESPEN guidelines [1].

Results: 32 patients were included (prone n = 16). Both groups were comparable in age, sex, comorbidities, biochemical markers and Nutrition Risk Screening on admission. Time on IMV was longer in the prone group (p = 0.032). The total time in prone position ranged from 19.5 h to 13.16 d. All patients received a NG tube, 1 proned patient received a jejunal tube. 6 received TPN (p = 0.654). Metoclopramide was used more often in the prone group (p = 0.028). The prevalence of vomiting (n = 4 vs. n = 5), large gastric residuals (n = 0 vs. n = 3) and VAP (n = 11 vs. n = 10) were comparable for the non-prone vs. prone group, resp. Table 1 shows the percentage of targets reached. These were lower in the prone group, though not statistically significant. However, when correcting for SAPS III-score, the impact of proning declined.

Conclusions: These limited data suggest there is no significant difference in feeding COVID-19 patients on IMV that need proning vs. those who do not, except for metoclopramide use. Overall, reaching nutrition targets in these patients is challenging. This model suggests that disease impact is a greater influence on reaching nutritional goals than proning itself.

list-item.p: Singer P et al. Clin Nutr 2019;38:48–79.

caption.p: Table 1 (abstract P329). Percentage of targets reached

sec.title: Comparative evaluation of different respiratory rehabilitation methods in the early postoperative period of cardiac surgery patients
DV Fomina, AA Eremenko, TP Zyulyaeva, MS Grekova, AP Alferova, OO Grin, SS Dmitrieva
Petrovsky National Research Centre of Surgery, intensive care unit, Moscow, Russian Federation
sec.p: Critical Care 2023, 27(S1): P330

Introduction: The frequency of postoperative respiratory failure due to ineffective coughing and evacuation of bronchial mucus is 17%-22%. The objective was to evaluate the effectiveness of different cough stimulation methods in the early postoperative period of cardiac surgery patients.

Methods: In randomized prospective comparative study (ClinicalTrials.gov—NCT05159401) four methods were evaluated. There were 20 patients in each group. 1st group—oscillatory PEP-therapy (Acapella DH Green, Portex), group 2—external chest oscillatory massage combined with intermittent pressure mask ventilation Comfort Cough Plus, Seoil Pacific Corporation, (CC), group 3—vibro-acoustic lung massage, BARK VibroLUNG, (VL). The 4th control group included patients with manual chest massage (MCM). Procedures were performed 10–12 h after tracheal extubation 3 times a day for 3 days. Before the procedure and 20 min after it, the effectiveness of sputum discharge, gas exchange and maximum inspiratory lung capacity (MILC) were evaluated.

Results: The largest number of patients with productive cough was fixed in groups 1, 2 and 4 after fourth session, in group VL during 4–6 session. The increase in SpO2 on room air after 3 days of treatment with PEP-therapy and MCM was 4%, in others—5% (Table 1). The maximum increase in MILC was registered in the patients with PEP-therapy, the minimum—in the MCM group. Mean MILC exceeded more than 2000 ml in group 1 by session 7, in group 2 by session 4, in group 3 by session 9, in group 4 by session 8. No significant effect on PaCO2 was noted.

Conclusions: The effects of the applied methods of mechanical stimulation of cough were directed to improve the passage of mucus and oxygenating lung function. After 3 days of treatment the most significant increase of MILC was registered in PEP-therapy and CC group. No undesirable effects of the procedures were noted.

sec.title: The effect of physical rehabilitation on ICU adults
C Chatzimatthaiou1, F Moniati2
1Barts and The London, School of Medicine, London, UK, 2Barts and The London, London, UK
sec.p: Critical Care 2023, 27(S1): P331

Introduction: A large percentage of critical illness survivors suffer due to loss of muscle mass, polyneuropathy and myopathy which occur during the early phase of ICU admission and are correlated with increased ICU and hospital stays. In order to manage this physical sequela of critical illness, a specific approach has been adopted: physical rehabilitation. In the ICU, rehabilitation is used to reverse muscle catabolism, reduce the effects of immobility and improve physical functioning. Our main outcome is to investigate the effect of physical rehabilitation in ICU specifically on the outcomes of patients, the dose–response profile and the impact it has on task-specific training.

Methods: We conducted a systematic review using the Ovid Medline, Cochrane and Embase databases to explore the current studies available on physical rehabilitation in the ICU. We included all randomized controlled trials which investigated physical rehabilitation in the ICU in adults. Physical function, muscle strength, hospital and ICU stays, health-related quality of life and mortality comprised our outcomes.

Results: We included sixty-five trials, with a total of 5658 participants. Results showed that physical rehabilitation decreased hospital length stay by 1.77 days (mean difference, 1.77 d: 95% CI 3.05–0.48 d), ICU length of stay by 0.7 days (mean difference, 0.70 d: 95% CI 1.39 TO 0.25 d) and physical function at the hospital discharge has been improved (mean difference, 0.24; 95% CI0.00–0.46). In trials which investigated functional exercises and low-dose physical rehabilitation, the intervention was more effectual compared to the control. Lastly, there was no impact on the other outcomes caused by physical rehabilitation.
Conclusions: We concluded that physical rehabilitation in the ICU overall decreased the hospital and ICU length of stay and improved physical function.

sec.title: Impact of early rehabilitation on the functional quality of life of the critically ill at one year
ARA Rodríguez Alcaraz1, BLV Lobo Valbuena1, LMC Manglano-Cerrato2, AA Alcina2, RML Molina Lobo1, PB Benavides2, JJ Juárez2, RCV Castañeda Vozmediano3, FG Gordo1

1Hospital del Henares, Servicio de Medicina Intensiva, Coslada, Madrid, Spain, 2Hospital del Henares, Servicio de Medicina Física y Rehabilitación, Coslada, Madrid, Spain, 3Facultad de Ciencias de la Salud, Universidad Francisco de Vitoria, Grupo de Investigación en Patología Crítica, Pozuelo de Alarcón, Madrid, Spain
sec.p: Critical Care 2023, 27(S1): P332

Introduction: ICU-acquired weakness (ICUAW) is a long-recognised phenomenon, featuring a prevalence of 25–80%. Early mobilisation is anaccepted intervention that may attenuate ICUAW and improve outcomes [1, 2].

Methods: Prospective observational study in polyvalent ICU analysing the effect of early rehabilitation (eRHB) on quality of life one year after discharge (D/C).Patients who required invasive mechanical ventilation > 24 h and survived SARS-CoV2 respiratory infection between 5/3/2020 and 12/01/2022 were included. Patients were classified into two groups: eRHB or not eRHB. Demographic and clinical data were collected, and a telephone survey was conducted one year after D/C. Clinical Frailty Scale at ICU admission (T1) and one year after D/C (T5); Medical Research Council (MRC) at the start of rehabilitation (T2) and hospital D/C (T4); Barthel Index at ICU D/C (T3), T4 and T5; and the SF-36 health questionnaire at T5 were also collected. Statistical analysis was performed between subgroups: Pearson's Chi-square test or Mann–Whitney U test to find significant differences. ART-ANOVA was used to analyse the survey results.

Results: Of 99 patients, 64.6% belonged to the eRHB group. There were no statistically significant differences in the analysis of clinic-demographic variables. We observed a significant improvement of the MRC, a better Barthel Index in the eRHB group, and a statistically significant positive impact on several components of the SF-36 in the eRHB group (physical functioning, vitality, social functioning, bodily pain, general health, and self-reported health transition).

Conclusions: Patients who received eRHB had better physical functioning and higher vitality recovery. In addition, they suffered less impact on their social life, had better pain control, and reported improved general health. All this emphasises the need for eRHB protocols in the ICU, promoting multidisciplinary care of our patients.

list-item.p: Lang JK et al. Crit Care Med. 2020;48:e1121–e1128.

sec.title: Effect of early mobilization within 72 h of admission or intubation of critically ill ICU patients on length of ICU stay: a systematic review with meta-analysis
N Daum1, N Drewniok1, A Buyukli1, B Ulm2, J Grunow1, S Schaller1
1Charité – Universitätsmedizin Berlin, Department of Anesthesiology and Operative Intensive Care Medicine (CVK, CCM), Berlin, Germany, 2Technical University of Munich, Munich, Germany
sec.p: Critical Care 2023, 27(S1): P333

Introduction: Previous systematic reviews showed a positive effect of early mobilization on ICU length of stay (LOS). There is data showing that an early start as recommended, i.e., within 72 h after ICU admission or intubation in critically ill patients, is essential for positive effects on ICU LOS [1, 2]. Therefore, we aimed to investigate the effectiveness of early mobilization within 72 h of ICU admission or intubation.

Methods: We conducted a systematic review and meta-analysis examining the effect of early mobilization within 72 h compared with standard care in terms of ICU length of stay. Here, randomized controlled trials were included from inception to October 8, 2022. Early mobilization was defined, based on Bein et al., as any patient intervention that initiates and/or supports passive or active range of motion exercises and aims to promote and/or maintain range of motion [1].

Results: Of a total of 29,680 studies screened, 18 studies with a total of 1833 patients met the inclusion criteria and were included in the statistical analysis. In the meta-analysis, the random effects model showed a positive effect in ICU LOS in the group after admission in favor of early mobilization compared with standard care (WMD (weighted mean difference): −0.57 days, 95% CI [−1.26, 0.13]; SMD (standardized mean difference): −0.44, 95% CI [−0.82; −0.06]; 16 studies with 1567 patients; see Fig. 1). The effect after intubation proved to be nonsignificant (WMD: −0.65 days, 95% CI [−1.92, 0.63]; SMD: −0.37, 95% CI [−1.11; 0.38]; 2 studies with 266 patients). When all studies were considered together, there was a positive effect in favor of early mobilization compared with standard care with a significant SMD (WMD: −0.58 days, 95% CI [−1.19, 0.03]; SMD: −0.43, 95% CI [−0.78; −0.09]).
Conclusions: Early mobilization within 72 h of ICU admission or after intubation had a positive albeit small effect on LOS in the ICU.

list-item.p: Bein T et al. Anaesthesist 2015;64:596–611.

caption.p: Fig. 1 (abstract P333). Weighted mean difference of ICU LOS in relation to starting early mobilization within 72 h of ICU admission or intubation

sec.title: Improving patient outcomes-a challenging approach in discharge planning from intensive care to rehabilitation
A Goldenberg, Y Polishook, S Guetta
Kaplan Medical Center, General Intensive Care Unit, Rehovot, Israel
sec.p: Critical Care 2023, 27(S1): P334

Introduction: By improving the discharge process from intensive care to rehabilitation facility it is possible to improve patient outcomes while maintaining continuity of quality care [1]. Planning discharge early saves hospital beds, shortens patients overall hospitalization, and increases patient-family satisfaction.

Methods: A team discussion based on patients individual requirements.Involvement of multi-disciplinary team. Development of discharge checklist to include:all the relevant information required to guarantee continuity of treatment, contacting facility to coordinate discharge,checking and meeting all pre-admission criteria to facility. Obtaining relevant authorization from Health Care Authority. Early involvement of family. Preparation of treatment summaries, transferring all relevant information.recording NEWS 2 score. After discharge follow up call.

Results: 48 intensive care patients that were either transferred out of ICU to a ward, or discharged to a rehabilitation facility over a two year period from 21.11.2019 until 7.6.2022. All rehabilitation facilities reported increased satisfaction with the discharge process. Average time from decision to discharge to actual discharge, being shortened from 5 to 2 days.

Conclusions: By utilizing planned discharge we were able to successfully discharge our patients from intensive care unit to rehabilitation facility, thereby improving patient outcomes. We prevented readmissions, shortened hospital stay, led to a reduction in overall ICU stay and increased satisfaction on the part of both rehabilitation facility and family.

list-item.p: Mcneill H et al. JMIR Perioper Med 2020;3:e13782.

sec.title: Acute stress disorder and post traumatic stress disorder after ICU admission for acute respiratory failure due to COVID-19
G Salve1, G Ceschin2, C Sonnati2, M Moscariello2, M Forte2, DM Piovera1, M Mangiagalli1, S Mongodi1, F Mojoli1
1Policlinico San Matteo, Rianimazione I, Pavia, Italy, 2Policlinico San Matteo, Scuola di specializzazione Anestesia, Rianimazione, Pavia, Italy
sec.p: Critical Care 2023, 27(S1): P335

Introduction: ICU survivors may present symptoms of acute stress disorder(ASD) or post traumatic stress disorder (PTSD) [1], as defined by the Diagnostic and Statistical Manual of Mental Disorders. Prevalence of PTSD in ARDS affects 20% of survivors but is higher in SARS/MERS patients [2]; risk factors are delirium, ICU sedation, altered memories or ICU amnesia [3].

Methods: We enrolled patients discharged from our ICU (Pavia-Italy) after admission for acute respiratory failure due to COVID-19. Written consent was collected. Inclusion criteria: age ≥ 18 years, ICU admission between February 2020 and July 2021 for severe COVID-19, SARS-CoV-2 infection confirmed by positive nasal swab/tracheal aspirate, available follow-up visit. Exclusion criteria: refuse to attend the follow up visit, refuse to perform the psychological tests. To assess ASD/PTSD we used the IES-R: a total mean score ≥ 33 was the cut-off value for ASD/PTSD; subscale scores for avoidance, intrusion, hyperarousal were calculated. Quantitative and categorical variables are expressed as median [IQR] and number (%).

Results: Of the 491 patients admitted to ICU, 113 patients were included (males 84(74.3%), age 61.0[52.0–66.0] years, BMI 28.2[25.8–31.6] kg/m2, SAPS2 31.0[26.0–41.5], ICU stay 17.0[8.0–28.0] days, invasive mechanical ventilation 61(54.0%). They were assessed in median 107.0 [82.0–150.0] days after ICU discharge. 37 patients (32.7%) had ASD/PTSD, higher than the expected 20% in general ARDS population (p < 0.001). Both intrusion 11.0[5.0–17.0] and avoidance 7.0[3.0–13.0] median scores were higher than hyperarousal 5.0[2.0–9.0].

Conclusions: ASD/PTSD is frequent after discharge to ICU for severe COVID-19, this prevalence is higher than other ARDS population, maybe for stigma, difficulties with quarantine and isolation. Patients have higher intrusion and avoidance scores, maybe because during the pandemic isolation was unavoidable.

list-item.p: Brown SM et al. Ann Am Thorac Soc 2019;16:947–56.
Ahmed H et al. J Rehabil Med. 2020;52:jrm00063.
Righy C et al. Crit Care 2019;23:213.

sec.title: Analysis of mortality and long-term prognosis in ventilated patients with COVID-19: a multicenter prospective observational study
K Nakamura1, J Hatakeyama2

1Teikyo University Hospital, Department of Emergency and Critical Care Medicine, Tokyo, Japan, 2Osaka Medical and Pharmaceutical University, Department of Emergency and Critical Care Medicine, Osaka, Japan
sec.p: Critical Care 2023, 27(S1): P336

Introduction: Long-term prognosis, especially for post-intensive care syndrome (PICS) is an emerging problem in critically ill patients. Prevalence and risk factors are unclear in patients with severe coronavirus disease 2019 (COVID-19). We aimed to investigate the prevalence and risks of mortality and PICS in ventilated patients with COVID-19.

Methods: A multicenter prospective study was conducted on ventilated patients with COVID-19 infection. The questionnaire for PICS evaluation was mailed within a median of 6 mo after hospital discharge, concerning Barthel Index, Short-Memory Questionnaire, and Hospital Anxiety and Depression Scale scores.

Results: 251 patients completed the PICS questionnaires with a prevalence of PICS of 58.6%, along with the highest percentages of cognitive impairment. Delirium (OR 2.34, p = 0.03) and the duration of mechanical ventilation (OR 1.29, p = 0.02) were identified as independent risks for PICS. In 297 patients who received mechanicalventilation for 7 day or longer, protein and energy delivery in day 4–7, especially for protein delivery, were independently and monotonically associated with in-hospital mortality, but not with PICS occurence.

Conclusions: 60% of the ventilated patients with COVID-19 suffered from PICS. Delirium and longer mechanical ventilation were identified as risks for PICS. In the patents requiring longer mechanical ventilation, nutrition delivery in the late period of the acute phase might be imprtant to survive COVID-19.

sec.title: A retrospective review of the functional status of patients post-severe COVID-19 disease
S Angelov, S O’Keefe, C Sheehan, Z Aslam, B O’Brien
Cork University Hospital, Anaesthesiology & Intensive Care Medicine, Cork, Ireland
sec.p: Critical Care 2023, 27(S1): P337

Introduction: The purpose of this study was to evaluate the functional status and ongoing respiratory symptoms of patients who have been discharged home, who were admitted to the intensive care unit (ICU) with severe COVID-19 disease. Long term complications post critical illness, such as post intensive care syndrome (PICS), are well-recognised [1], however, little data exists specific to COVID-19 ICU survivors.

Methods: Local ethics committee approval was secured. Participants were recruited using the electronic intensive care chart database. All patients, from March 2020 to November 2021, who survived Covid-19 were included. Researchers conducted phone interviews with participants and completed three questionnaires; Katz Index of Independent Living Questionnaire, Karnofsky Performance Status Scale and the American Thoracic Society (ATS) Respiratory Disease Questionnaire. Data was input and analysed using excel and stored on a password encrypted computer.

Results: Database search revealed 61 patients available, 33 of 61 patients were included. 20 (61%) were male, 13 (39%) were female. Mean age was 58, with a range of 25–81. Mean length of ICU stay was 15 days, with a range of 1–74 days. 15 (45%) patients were intubated. Most common Katz score (range 1–6) was the highest score of 6 in 23 (70%) patients. Most common Karnofsky score (range 10–100) was 60 in 11 (33%) patients, with 31 (94%) scoring < 100. The most frequent ATS shortness of breath (SOB) grade (range 0–4) was 3 in 10 (9%) patients, with 23 (70%) patients scoring > 0. 14 (42%) patients had an ongoing cough, 11 (33%) patients had a wheeze and 0 (0%) patients were on home oxygen therapy.

Conclusions: This study highlights in the majority of patients who survived severe Covid-19 disease to discharge home, there were serious ongoing limitations to their functional status and exercise tolerance, however, they remained independent of basic activities of daily living.

list-item.p: Rawal G et al. J Transl Int Med 2017;5:90–92.

sec.title: The effect of consecutive COVID-19 waves on the mental wellbeing of ICU nursing staff
J Branders1, SN Noels2, MV Vreven2, GDH De Herdt2, HVE Vanden Eede3
1ZNA Middelheim, Intensive care, Antwerp, Belgium, 2University Hospital Antwerp, Anesthesiology, Edegem, Belgium, 3 AZ Rivierenland, Anesthesiology, Rumst, Belgium
sec.p: Critical Care 2023, 27(S1): P338

Introduction: Working on an ICU ward is considered a physical as well as mentally challenging profession which has already been objectified by multiple studies in the past. When the Covid-19 pandemic started, medical staff were suddenly exposed to even greater levels of stress not only at work but also due to social restrictions. Our primary aim of this cross-sectional study is to observe the evolution in time of the impact on mental health during as well as after the pandemic.

Methods: In this cross-sectional study the mental health of our ICU nursing staff (n = 18) was monitored over a period of 26 months by using validate questionnaires for anxiety, depression, insomnia and post-traumatic stress syndrome (PTSD) such as the Generalized Anxiety Disorder-7 (GAD-7) score, Patient Health Questionnaire-9 (PHQ-9), Insomnia severity index (ISI) and the revised version of the Impact of Event Severity scale (IES-r). In total it entailed 4 surveys during the pandemic and one follow up survey 6 months after.

Results: Regarding GAD7 and ISI, ICU nurses did not score significantly at the beginning nor the end of this study. IES scores started off at 18 which is just below significant but 35% had a score associated with developing PTSD. These scores remained relatively high throughout the pandemic but saw a mean downward trend at the end (p = 0.338). Mean PHQ-9 scores were initially at the level of “minimal depression” and rose to be classified as “mild depression” at the end of the pandemic. In the follow-up survey scores dropped back to “minimal depression”. In time, the PHQ-9 test was not withheld as significant (p = 0.298).

Conclusions: It is evident that the results presented in our study show that ICU nurses are prone to developing mental health problems during times of increased workload and stress and in particular the development of PTSD remained high in some subjects. Although there was a slight decrease in our follow-up survey, we believe mental health should be closely followed upon in the future.

sec.title: Potential benefits of automated infusion relay: survey of its possibilities from the material waste and cost perspective
E Meijer1, M Onrust1, W Dieperink1, F Doesburg2, W Bult3, M Nijsten4

1UMC Groningen, Dpt of Critical Care, Groningen, Netherlands, 2UMC Groningen, Dpt of Medical Technology, Groningen, Netherlands, 3UMC Groningen, Dpt of Clinical Pharmacy and Pharmacology, Groningen, Netherlands, 4UMC Groningen, ICU, Groningen, Netherlands
sec.p: Critical Care 2023, 27(S1): P339

Introduction: Manipulations with continuous syringe infusions in the ICU are time-consuming and cost-intensive. Nurses often have to exchange syringes at preplanned times, but pre-emptive swapping of syringes may result in considerable unused residual volume, and thus additional waste. During vasopressor infusion, automated infusion relay is usually considered for ensuring a smooth switching between syringe pumps. However, infusion relay also allows the optimal use of drug solutions and could present an opportunity to reduce environmental footprint and lower costs. Consequently, we studied the magnitude of unused drug disposal in our ICU in a context of manual syringe exchange, to evaluate the potential benefit of an automated infusion relay function.

Methods: We performed a prospective study over 3 weeks in a 32-bed tertiary university adult ICU. All patients, except strictly isolated patients (e.g. with COVID-19, MRSA or ESBL) were included. All 50 ml syringes that were disposed of before or after use were separately collected per patient and the constituent solution and the remaining volume were recorded. Medication costs, as used internally in our hospital, were identified for the 20 most commonly used drugs or solutions.

Results: A total of 2667 syringes from 105 subsequent adult ICU patients were collected and analyzed (Fig. 1). Expired medication or discontinuation of medication accounted for disposal of 179 (7%) completely filled syringes. The 2488 remaining syringes had a mean ± SD residual volume of 12 ± 13 ml (24%). This remaining medication volume had an associated cost of € 5736 or 28% of total cost of fully filled syringes, or at least €100,000 on an annual basis.

Conclusions: In our ICU approximately a quarter of syringe solutions are discarded during syringe switching. In terms of waste production, associated nursing time and associated material costs, more efficient syringe exchanges with automated infusion relay may address these three concerns.
Fig. 1 (abstract P339). Frequency distribution

sec.title: Identifying barriers to a robust second check of medication
V Milic, L Cameron, C Jones
St Thomas Hospital, Pharmacy, London, UK
sec.p: Critical Care 2023, 27(S1): P340

Introduction: Medication errors are common in critically ill patients with a reported average of 1.7 medical errors each day [1]. Critically ill patients are at increased risk of medication error and patient harm, due to complex medication regimens with frequent changes, organ dysfunction increasing potential for harm from medication errors, and greater use of intravenous medications are associated with increased errors. Many hospitals have policies mandating an independent second check for high risk medication administration such as intravenous drugs or Controlled Drugs, with the intention of improving safety by identifying and preventing errors reaching the patient. This second checking process has a significant time implications and the efficacy for error prevention is debated with adherence to second checking being documented between 52 and 72% within literature [2, 3]. The aim of this study was to determine the barriers for a robust independent second check of medication within adult critical care.

Methods: Four focus groups were conducted with critical care nurses at Guy’s and St Thomas’ Foundation Trust. Thematic analysis was undertaken to identify potential barriers to implementing an effective second check. Themes were then analysed for thematic content by three critical care pharmacists and grouped with evidence to support or refute the themes.
Results: Subthemes and themes are described in Table 1. The main themes identified as barriers to an independent second checking were complex processes, IT, routine, personnel and geography.

Conclusions: Our study identified a number of common themes associated with failures to implement an effective second check for medication in a critical care. The main barriers identified were personnel, geography and routine. Overall, ethnographic studies are required at ward level as a bigger piece of research to try to further understand second checking.

list-item.p: Moyen E et al. Crit Care 2008;12:208.
Schilp J et al. BMJ Open 2014;4:e005232.
Alsulami Z et al., J Adv Nurs 2014;70:1404–13.

caption.p: Table 1 (abstract P340). Themes and subthemes identified from 29 nurses across 4 focus groups

tr.td: Timing of medication, varying IT systems
Protecting self, seniority of staff, staff

sec.title: Implementation of clinical pharmacy services in the ICU
FD Duchi
AZ Groeninge Kortrijk, Hospital Pharmacy, Kortrijk, Belgium
sec.p: Critical Care 2023, 27(S1): P341

Introduction: Because of the severity of the disease, the use of high-risk medication, the high frequency of changes in medication charts, and polymedication, patients admitted to the ICU are at high risk for the occurrence of clinically relevant drug-related problems (DRPs). One way to reduce the risk of DRPs is the presence of a hospital pharmacist in the ICU. Since Belgian healthcare is organized differently and the presence of a hospital pharmacist in ICU teams is not well established, a study was conducted to implement clinical pharmacy services.

Methods: A prospective non-controlled interventional six-week study was conducted in a 31-bed intensive care unit in az groeninge Kortrijk, Belgium. The hospital pharmacist was involved in the evaluation of the medication chart of the patient, attending medical rounds, and answering drug-related questions. Clinical interventions were presented to physicians. The acceptance rate was measured, and potential risk factors associated with the acceptance rate were identified.

Results: A total of 204 DRPs were detected. Physicians accepted 158 interventions, resulting in an acceptance rate of 77.5%. Statistical analysis showed that the acceptance rate was significantly associated with the type of proposed intervention. The intervention to change a drug and to pause or stop a drug was implemented more frequently.

Conclusions: Providing patient care to critically ill patients by a multidisciplinary team is an emphasis of the modern day ICU because the team approach is invaluable. Through proactive participation in evaluating the appropriateness of the medication chart, identifying and reporting DRPs, and proposing evidence-based interventions, the pharmacist contributed to the multidisciplinary team in the ICU. Quality improvement by the implementation of clinical pharmacy resulted in high acceptance rates of proposed interventions and high appreciation for the presence of a hospital pharmacist from health care providers in the ICU.

sec.title: Adult admissions to an intensive care unit due to adverse drug reactions
G Masiliūnienė1, E Kaduševičius2, E Stankevičius2
1Kaunas Hospital of the Lithuanian University of Health Sciences, ICU, Kaunas, Lithuania, 2Lithuanian University of Health Sciences, Lithuanian University of Health Sciences, Kaunas, Lithuania
sec.p: Critical Care 2023, 27(S1): P342

Introduction: Adverse drug reactions (ADRs) remain a huge burden on health care, leading to significant morbidity, mortality, and costs. Based on different literature resources, the prevalence of ADR-related hospital admissions ranges from 0.2 to 54.5% [1]. The costs of an ADR have been reported to be substantial with an additional $6000–$9000 for each event [2]. The aim of this study was to determine the frequency, probability, and severity of ADRs and ADR-related costs in patients admitted to the ICU.

Methods: This prospective consecutive study was carried out in the ICU of Kaunas Hospital of the Lithuanian University of Health Sciences from September 2021 to November 2022. Adult patients admitted to the ICU because of an ADR were included. The probability of suspected ADRs was evaluated using the Naranjo Scale. The severity of ADRs was assessed by the Modified Hartwig and Siegel Scale.

Results: A total of 768 patients were admitted to the ICU during a 15-month period. Of them, 43 (5.6%) admissions were related to ADRs. The mean age of patients admitted due to ADRs was 76.7 years (SD 13.8; range, 33–97). Most of the suspected ADRs (74.5%) were classified as probable; 16.3%, as definite; and 9.3%, as possible. The majority (93%) of ADRs were categorized as severe (of them, 32.5% resulting in death) and the remaining ADRs were moderate. The most commonly reported ADR was bleeding (53.5%) followed by hyponatremia and bradycardia (each 14%), diarrhea (11.6%) and other such as coma, pulmonary edema, etc. (7%). The costs per event ranged from €502 to €11,280. Health care expenditures attributed to ADR-related admissions to the ICU totaled more than €172,000.

Conclusions: Our study showed that the overwhelming majority of ADRs were severe and one-third of them led to a fatal outcome. This warrants future studies on preventability and solutions how to reduce the costs of ADR-related ICU admissions.

list-item.p: Angamo MT et al. Drug Saf 2016;39:847–57.
Kane-Gill SL et al. Crit Care Med 2010;38:S83–9.

sec.title: Improving drug preparation during emergencies and rapid sequence induction in the adult intensive care unit
I Canisius, A Brown, C Lambert
St Mary’s Hospital, Adult Intensive Care, London, UK
sec.p: Critical Care 2023, 27(S1): P343

Introduction: We aimed to improve efficiency and safety in preparing emergency and rapid sequence induction drugs (ERDs). Critically ill patients are exposed to multiple drugs, and the fast-paced ICU environment is an accepted contributor towards error [1]. Emergencies and intubation involve particularly high-risk injectables, and errors may have devastating effects. Drug preparation may be delegated to doctors of varying experience, causing potential error or delay. We instituted dedicated ERD preparation stations containing drugs, equipment and a quick-reference table. As per European guidance [2], we introduced pre-filled syringes of drugs that would otherwise need dilution, to avoid miscalculation and extra steps.

Methods: Efficiency and safety in ERD preparation was assessed pre- and post-intervention. Doctors were surveyed regarding knowledge and confidence levels. Two subsets of 10 doctors completed a practical task pre- and post- intervention to record the time taken to prepare a full set of ERDs. They were each given a drug reference table to mitigate prior knowledge as an influencing variable. Participants completed feedback to identify equipment and process factors that they felt caused delays or risk of error.

Results: 20 pre- and 11 post-intervention survey responses were collected. 91% of responders felt that the new stations had improved safety and efficiency. There was a 61% increase (p = 0.003) in awareness of drug locations post-intervention. In the practical assessments, the average time taken to prepare a full set of drugs decreased from 20 to 7 min pre- to post-intervention (p = 0.00009).

Conclusions: Dedicated drug preparation stations have improved efficiency and drug location knowledge. The stations will be extended to other trust ICUs, where we aim to re-audit with a larger sample. Quantifying impact on drug error rate and associated avoidable patient harm requires longer-term study.
Acknowledgement: I. Canisius and A. Brown contributed equally to the study as first author.

list-item.p: Kane-Gill S et al. Crit Care Clin 2006;22:273–290.
Whitaker D et al. Eur J Anaesthesiol 2017;34:4–7.

sec.title: Prevalence of frailty in critically ill cancer patients and its impact on acute outcomes and post ICU discharge quality of life
SK Sundaramurthy1, R Dominic Savio1, S Thomas1, L Ranganathan2, R Venkataraman2, N Ramakrishnan2, DD Vidyasagar3

1Apollo Proton Cancer Centre, Critical Care Medicine, Chennai, India, 2Apollo Hospital, Critical Care Medicine, Chennai, India, 3Apollo Cancer Hospital, Teynampet, Critical Care Medicine, Chennai, India
sec.p: Critical Care 2023, 27(S1): P344

Introduction: The impact of frailty on critically ill cancer patients remains to be elucidated. We sought to assess its prevalence in this cohort and its impact on acute outcomes and 90-day post hospital discharge health related quality of life (HRQoL).

Methods: Single centre, prospective, cohort study conducted in the intensive care unit (ICU) of a tertiary care, cancer hospital. All consecutive adult patients with cancer, admitted to ICU over a 10 months period were included in the study. Patients on end-of-life care were excluded. Frailty was assessed upon ICU admission using the Clinical Frailty Scale (CFS version 2.0) by two independent investigators [1]. Data pertaining to demographics and outcomes were documented. The acute outcomes analysed were requirement for respiratory and vasopressor support, time to mobilization, ICU readmission, ICU length of stay (LOS), hospital LOS and in-hospital mortality. Patients were followed-up at 90 days post hospital discharge with a phone interview to assess their HRQoL using the EuroQol 5D 5L questionnaire [2].

Results: A total of 516 patients were included in the study. All of them were diagnosed to have frailty (77.9% mild). There was moderate inter-observer agreement on CFS (0.552 kappa coefficient). Advanced age (p = 0.046) and malnutrition (p = 0.027) were found to be significant risk factors.
