All acute outcomes, namely, requirement for respiratory (p < 0.01) and vasopressor (p < 0.01) support, time to mobilization (p = 0.01), ICU readmission (p < 0.01), ICU LOS (p < 0.01), hospital LOS (p < 0.01) and in-hospital mortality (p = 0.01) were adversely affected by frailty. Patients with higher frailty reported significantly poorer health scores (p = 0.01) and had worse performance among four out of the five dimensions in EQ 5D 5L at 90 days.
Conclusions: Critically ill cancer patients have a high prevalence of frailty and this adversely affects acute outcomes and 90-day post discharge HRQoL.

list-item.p: Rockwood K et al. Can Geriatr J. 2020;23:210–215.
Janssen MF et al. Qual Life Res 2013;22:1717–1727.

sec.title: Impact of frailty on long-term quality of life in patients undergoing cardiac surgery
M Schroyens1, SM Bagshaw1, CM Norris2, DB Rolfson3, SR Meyer4, HT Stelfox5, MA Zibdawi1, CL Montgomery2

1University of Alberta, Department of Critical Care Medicine, Edmonton, Canada, 2University of Alberta, Faculty of Nursing, Edmonton, Canada, 3University of Alberta, Division of Geriatric Medicine, Department of Medicine, Edmonton, Canada, 4University of Alberta, Division of Cardiac Surgery, Department of Surgery, Edmonton, Canada, 5University of Calgary, Department of Critical Care Medicine, Cumming School of Medicine, Calgary, Canada
sec.p: Critical Care 2023, 27(S1): P345

Introduction: Frailty is associated with a risk of worse outcomes and higher health services use in adult patients undergoing cardiac surgery. In this study we assessed the impact of baseline frailty status on health-related quality of life (HRQL) at 12 months following cardiac surgery.

Methods: This prospective cohort study included patients aged 50 years and older who underwent non-emergent surgery between November 2011 and March 2014 in two adult cardiac surgery programs in Alberta, Canada. Frailty was defined as a Clinical Frailty Scale (CFS) score of 5 or greater. HRQL was assessed using the EuroQol 5-domain 3-level (EQ-5D) and Short Form-12 (SF-12) surveys in person pre-operatively and by telephone at 6 months and at 12 months after surgery. Patient socio-demographics, comorbidities, surgery details, cardiac surgery risk scores, adverse events and discharge disposition were also collected.

Results: The cohort (529 patients) mean (SD) age was 67 (9) years. 25.9% were female and the pre-operative prevalence of frailty was 9.6%. Patients with frailty were older (median [IQR] age 75 [65–80] v. 67 [60–73], p < 0.001) and had higher EuroSCORE II (8 [6–9] v. 5 [3–7], p < 0.001). SF-12 physical component score increased from baseline to 12-months in frail (27.5 [9.4]–38.2 [11.9], p < 0.001) and non-frail patients (37.0 [11.4]–47.3 [9.9], p < 0.001). Mental component score in patients with frailty was similar (47.7 [12.0]–52.2 [10.4], p = 0.29) and increased in non-frail (51.8 [10.7]–54.9 [8.5], p < 0.001). Mean (SD) EQ-5D VAS increased in frail (46.2 [18.9]–60.3 [21.6], p < 0.001) and non-frail (60.2 [20.2]–76.6 [15.4], p < 0.001).

Conclusions: HRQL was more impaired in patients with frailty at baseline although incremental improvement in HRQL was reported in frail and non-frail patients over 12-months post-operatively. Recognizing the risk to HRQL among patients with frailty pre-operatively requires further exploration to identify potential interventions to optimize outcomes.

sec.title: Validity of physician-perceived frailty in the intensive care unit
Y Angel, O Marudi, A Nini, D Stavi, A Nevo, D Aviram, Y Lichter, N Adi, I Matot, A Gal-Oz
Tel Aviv Sourasky Medical Center, Division of Anesthesia, Pain Management and Intensive Care, Tel Aviv, Israel
sec.p: Critical Care 2023, 27(S1): P346

Introduction: Frailty is a valuable predictor for outcome in elderly intensive care unit (ICU) patients. Physicians in ICU often describe patients as “frail” in reference to their pre-morbid condition and possibly their chances of recovery following ICU admission. Several validated scores to assess frailty have been developed, such as the Clinical Frailty Scale (CFS) and the Modified Frailty Index (MFI). These scores correlate well with post-operative and post-ICU mortality [1–3]. Nonetheless, in our clinical experience, ICU physicians more often opt to use their clinical judgement, or “eyeball assessment”, to estimate the degree of frailty.

Methods: A retrospective study of patients admitted to a single center ICU in a large academic medical center in Tel Aviv during March and April 2019. Physician-perceived frailty, as reported on admission on an ordinal scale of no frailty, mild, moderate, or severe frailty, was compared with results of validated scores such as CFS and MFI calculated retrospectively from patient records.

Results: 100 consecutive patients were included; 60 [60%] were male, mean age 58 ± 18 years. The Spearman Correlation test measured a correlation of 0.31 (p = 0.002) between physician-perceived frailty and CFS and 0.1 (p = 0.326) between physician-perceived frailty and MFI. On multivariate logistic regression, physician-perceived frailty was found to be independently associated with 30-day mortality (OR 2.16, p < 0.001; Fig. 1) but not with the duration of mechanical ventilation, ICU or hospital stay.

Conclusions: In this single-center, retrospective study, no correlation was found between physician-perceived frailty and CFS or MFI scores. Nevertheless, physician-perceived level of frailty was significantly associated with 30-day mortality. Larger and prospective studies are needed to ascertain these results.

list-item.p: Chen X et al. Clin Interv Aging. 2014;9:433–41.
Dasgupta M et al. Arch Gerontol Geriatr. 2009;48:78–83.
Le Maguet P et al. Intensive Care Med. 2014;40:674–82.

caption.p: Fig. 1 (abstract P346). Kaplan-Meier curve showing 30 day survival stratified by physician-perceived frailty score on admission

sec.title: Frailty among critically ill adults in India and association with patient-centered outcomes: a registry embedded cohort study
BK Tirupakuzhi Vijayaraghavan, IRIS Frailty Investigators
Apollo Main Hospital, Department of Critical Care Medicine, Chennai, India
sec.p: Critical Care 2023, 27(S1): P347

Introduction: There are limited data on frailty and outcomes for critically ill patients in low/middle income settings. We studied the prevalence of frailty at admission and evaluated association with patient-centred outcomes.

Methods: Prospective registry-embedded cohort study conducted in 7 Indian ICUs (4 hospitals). Adult patients anticipated to stay for ≥ 48 h were included. Exposure of interest was frailty as defined by the Clinical Frailty Scale (> 5) and the primary outcome was ICU mortality. Additional outcomes were in-hospital mortality, receipt of invasive or non-invasive ventilation or kidney replacement therapy, stage 2/3 acute kidney injury (AKI), and length of ICU stay. Categorical outcomes were compared using Chi-square and continuous outcomes using Mann–Whitney test. For analyses of ICU and hospital mortality, we employed generalized linear models; associations are reported as odds ratios (OR) and 95% confidence intervals (CI).

Results: 838 patients were included. Median (IQR) age was 57 (42.68) years and 65.0% were male. The prevalence of frailty was 166/838 (20.0%). The median (IQR) APACHE II among frail patients was 15(10–20) as compared to 11(7–16) among the non-frail and median (IQR) Charlson comorbidity score was 3(1–4) among frail patients as compared to 1(0–3) among the non-frail. Frailty was associated with ICU mortality (adjusted OR1.91, 95% CI 1.30–3.11), hospital mortality (adjusted OR 2.22, 1.44–3.39), receipt of non-invasive ventilation (unadjusted OR 2.68, 1.77–4.03), receipt of kidney replacement therapy (unadjusted OR 3.15, 1.90–5.17), receipt of vasopressors (unadjusted OR 1.47, 1.04–2.07), and stage 2/3 AKI (unadjusted OR 2.35, 1.60–3.43) (Fig. 1). There was no association with invasive ventilation (unadjusted OR 1.11, 0.71–1.57). Frail patients stayed longer in the ICU (median 6 days vs. 4 days, p < 0.001).

Conclusions: Frailty is common among critically ill patients in India and associated with worse outcomes.
Fig. 1 (abstract P347). Forest plot of outcomes for frail vs. non-frail critically ill adults

sec.title: Prognosis of chronic critical illness (CCI)/persistentinflammation immunosuppression and catabolism (PICS)
Q Zhou, J Liu
The Affiliated Suzhou Hospital of Nanjing Medical University, Suzhou Municipal Hospital, Suzhou Clinical Medical Center of Critical Care Medicine, Department of Critical Care Medicine, Suzhou, China
sec.p: Critical Care 2023, 27(S1): P348
Introduction: To investigate and compare the clinical features and prognosis of chronic critical illness (CCI)/persistent inflammation immunosuppression and catabolism (PICS).

Methods: This is a prospective observational clinical study. During this study period, we collect ICU patients’ data from Suzhou Municipal Hospital and Suzhou Ninth People’s Hospital. All patients older than 18 years of age were included and according to the corresponding exclusion and diagnostic criteria, they were divided into four groups: PICS group, CCI group, CCI and PICS group (CCI + PICS), nor CCI and nor PICS group (NCCI + NPICS), collected and recorded age, sex, hospital time, hospital diagnosis, acute physiological and chronic health status score II (APACHE II), sequential organ failure detection score(SOFA), c-reactive protein(CRP), absolute value lymphocyte count (L),serum albumin (Alb), white cells count (WBC), absolute value neutrophils count (N), secondary infection and 28-days case fatality rate separately.

Results: A total of 687 patients were admitted to the ICU during the study period. The hospitalization time less than 14 days were excluded and 168 patients were eventually included. There are 17 in the PICS group,71 in the CCI group,50 in the CCI + PICS group and 30 in the NCCI + NPICS group. Baseline characteristics showed statistically significant differences in SOFA, length of hospital stay, 28-day mortality among four groups. Baseline, main indicators and multiple comparisons showed that the CCI + PICS group had longer hospital stay,worse prognosis and more adverse outcomes. Multivariate analysis showed that final age, CRP on day 14 and 21, serum albumin on day 1 and 21 had an impact on the prognosis (p < 0.05).

Conclusions: The clinical prognosis of the four groups decreased in order of NCCI + NPICS, CCI, PICS, CCI + PICS. Our finding of clinically isolated PICS may indicate that PICS acts as a trigger or independent factor to worsen the prognosis of CCI.

sec.title: New-onset mental illness and long-term survival in survivors of critical illness: a population-based cohort study in South Korea
HY Jo, TK Oh, JW Hwang
Seoul National University Bundang Hospital, Anesthesiology and Pain Medicine, Seongnamsi, South Korea
sec.p: Critical Care 2023, 27(S1): P349

Introduction: Intensive care unit (ICU) survivors are at higher risk of developing mental illness. We aimed to examine the prevalence and associated factors of newly developed mental illness among ICU survivors of critical illness. Moreover, we examined the association between newly developed mental illness and the 2-years all-cause mortality.

Methods: All adult patients (≥ 20 years) who were admitted to the ICU during hospitalization between 2010 and 2018 and survived for 1-year were defined as ICU survivors and were included in this nationwide population-based cohort study. ICU survivors with a history of mental illness before ICU admission were excluded from the study. Depression, anxiety disorder, bipolar disorder, insomnia disorder, and substance abuse were considered as mental illnesses.

Results: A total of 1,316,776 ICU survivors were included in the analysis, and 21,274 survivors (1.6%) had newly developed mental illness within 1-year of ICU admission. Old age, longer ICU stay, increased Charlson comorbidity index, hospital admission through the emergency room, increased total cost of hospitalization, mechanical ventilator support, extracorporeal membrane oxygenation support, and continuous renal replacement therapy were associated with an increased prevalence of newly developed mental illness. Moreover, the newly developed mental illness group showed a 1.71-fold higher 2-years all-cause mortality rate than the no mental illness group (hazard ratio: 1.71; 95% confidence interval: 1.65–1.77; p < 0.001).

Conclusions: In South Korea, 1.6% of ICU survivors had newly developed mental illness within 1-year of ICU admission. Moreover, newly developed mental illness was associated with an increased 2-year all-cause mortality.

sec.title: Use of healthcare before and after sepsis: a case control study
J Dahlberg1, A Linder1, L Mellhammar2
1Lund University, Lund, Sweden, 2Lund University, Department of Clinical Sciences, Lund, Sweden
sec.p: Critical Care 2023, 27(S1): P350

Introduction: The aim of this study was to compare prevalence of readmissions and death between sepsis and non-sepsis hospitalizations the first year after discharge, and to investigate what diagnoses sepsis-patients present with at readmission. The aim was also to evaluate to what degree patients hospitalized for sepsis seek medical attention prior to hospitalization.

Methods: This was a retrospective case–control study with data validated through clinical chart review. A disproportionate stratified sampling model was used to include a relatively larger number of sepsis hospitalizations. All eight public hospitals in region Scania, Sweden were included (January 1 to December 31, 2019). 447 patients hospitalized for sepsis (cases), and 541 patients hospitalized for other causes (control) were identified through clinical chart review. Logistic regression was used to analyze healthcare the week prior to hospitalization, and cox regression was used to analyze death and readmission the year after discharge.

Results: 48% of patients that survived a sepsis hospitalization were readmitted the year after discharge, compared to 39% for non-sepsis patients (HR 1.50, 95% CI 1.03–2.19), p = 0.04 (Fig. 1). 32% of readmissions were caused by infection, and 18% by cardiovascular disease. 52% of readmissions occurred within 90 days and 75% within 180 days. Finally, 34% of sepsis patients had sought prehospital contact with a physician the week before hospitalization, compared to 22% for non-sepsis patients (OR 1.80, 95% CI 1.06–3.04), p = 0.03.

Conclusions: Patients hospitalized for sepsis had higher risk of readmission the year after discharge compared to non-sepsis patients. The most common diagnoses at readmission were infection followed by cardiovascular disease. With better follow up, some of these readmissions could potentially be prevented. Patients hospitalized for sepsis had sought prehospital contact the week prior to hospitalization to a greater extent than non-sepsis patients.
Fig. 1 (abstract P350). Unadjusted survival function for readmissions the year after discharge, with separate lines for sepsis and non-sepsis hospitalizations

sec.title: Resident-led simulation-based course increases first-year medical residents’ confidence in managing common critical care scenarios
M Vanden Eede1, H Vanden Eede2, D De Blick3, N Van Regenmortel4, K Monsieurs3, P Mertens5

1Universitair Ziekenhuis Antwerpen, Intensive Care, Edegem, Belgium, 2AZ Rivierland, Anesthesiologie, Reet, Belgium, 3Universitair Ziekenhuis Antwerpen, Emergency Department, Edegem, Belgium, 4ZNA Stuivenberg, Intensive Care, Antwerpen, Belgium, 5Universitair Ziekenhuis Antwerpen, Anesthesiologie, Edegem, Belgium
sec.p: Critical Care 2023, 27(S1): P351

Introduction: Simulation-based education (SBE) allows First-Year Residents (FYR) to practice their clinical, cognitive, and behavioral skills without having to deal with actual patients. This creates a secure learning environment where mistakes can be made safely [1]. SBE is commonly organized by medical faculties and includes feedback from experienced educators. The main disadvantage is that medical simulations are resource intensive, posing significant organizational challenges due to limited staff availability, compromising training opportunities [2]. In order to improve SBE delivery possibilities, senior residents could be engaged as teachers. We evaluated the effect of a resident-led SBE program on the confidence level of FYR to manage common critical care scenarios.

Methods: This study included FYR from internal medicine, anesthesiology and emergency medicine. The course was voluntary and encompassed an online module, followed by 4-h simulation training containing eight common critical care scenarios. Senior residents (third year or higher) who facilitated the training received a one-day training on didactic principles. FYR self-confidence was measured before and after the course and rated on a scale from 0 to 100% using a validated questionnaire [3].

Results: Forty FYR participated in the course. Survey data indicated a significant improvement in the confidence to lead resuscitations and to take care of common critical situations as depicted in Table 1 below.

Conclusions: A resident-led course that consisted of an online module and four hours of simulation training significantly improved FYR confidence in leading resuscitations and handling common critical care scenarios.

list-item.p: Issenberg SB et al. JAMA. 1999;282(9):861–6.
Seethamraju RR et al. Simul Healthc. 2022;17:e113–121.
Arnold JJ et al. Clin Sim Nurs. 2009;5:e35–34.

caption.p: Table 1 (abstract P351). Wilcoxon signed-rank test comparison of median confidence of first-year residents in leading resuscitations and taking care of common critical care scenarios before and after a resident-led simulation course

sec.title: Simulation-based clinical procedure education in the ICU
M Akatsuka, H Tatsumi, Y Masuda
Sapporo Medical University School of Medicine, Department of Intensive Care Medicine, Sapporo, Japan
sec.p: Critical Care 2023, 27(S1): P352

Introduction: The recent emphasis on hands-on teaching and training to develop professionalism for medical students and residents has placed significant demands on educational institutions for medicine. The traditional method of transmitting specialized knowledge through role modeling when educating students on clinical procedures has given rise to issues such as inadequate understanding and difficulty in maintaining motivation. Therefore, we focus on simulation-based education and group discussion. The aim of this research was to evaluate the state of training and clarify future issues.

Methods: Simulation-based education was performed for medical students and residents and a questionnaire survey was conducted to determine the impact the training had on them. The research was performed between January 2022 and October 2022. We have the following four steps: classroom learning; simulation-based education giving the roles of surgeon, caregiver and instructor; group discussions; and summary with the supervising physician.

Results: A total of 75 medical students and 5 residents participated, and all of the participants reported that the simulation exercise was useful. When residents performed the procedure on an actual patient after the simulation education, 80% of the residents were successful in their first attempt. Most of the post-procedure comments were positive, such as participants having “more confidence in the procedure” and “a clear understanding of the points of procedure”. However, there were some negative comments about anxiety due to the difference between the simulator and a patient.

Conclusions: The results suggest the need for future clinical procedure education to be conducted comprehensively through classroom lectures, simulation education, and practice. A faculty development programme designed to support the teaching and evaluation for faculty members would be helpful for medical students and residents to gain a deeper understanding of clinical procedures and acquire practical skills for patients.

sec.title: Implementing peer support in our intensive care unit to improve the mental wellbeing of trainees
M Quance, B Gay, E Howe, E Harlow, S Jarvis
Royal Cornwall Hospital, Critical Care, Truro, UK
sec.p: Critical Care 2023, 27(S1): P353

Introduction: Working on the intensive care unit (ICU) exposes staff to the suffering of patients and their families as a consequence of devastating illnesses and injuries, often leading to psychological trauma. The COVID-19 pandemic has amplified pressures on healthcare staff, resulting in deteriorating mental health and increased incidence of stress and burn out. The Intensive Care Society (ICS) has placed emphasis on peer support to combat this; recognising that working in a ‘psychologically safe’ environment improves wellbeing, enables safe and compassionate care, and reduces mistakes [1]. The aim of peer support is to improve wellbeing by offering a systematic, strategic approach to sustain staff who are coping well and provide initial support to those who are struggling.

Methods: Staff who have undertaken ICS training, under the supervision of a clinical psychologist, facilitate fortnightly meetings open to all ICU trainees. They also act as an initial contact to provide advice and guidance on addressing the psychological difficulties highlighted above. An anonymous screening questionnaire at the start of each session allows a moment of reflection for trainees prior to discussion. This, in addition to anonymous feedback, provides qualitative data to both audit the programme and inform improvement.

Results: Participation in peer support is voluntary, nonetheless attendance at meetings has been excellent. Initial qualitative results indicate that most trainees feel ‘nervous, anxious or on edge’, ‘question their capability at work’ and are ‘unable to stop thinking about work/a significant event at work’ at least a few days per week. Feedback from trainees regarding the programme has been intensely positive.

Conclusions: Anxiety and stress are high amongst ICU trainees which can lead to psychological harm if not addressed. Engagement from trainees in our programme has been good and feedback positive, emphasising the need for peer support in the ICU.

list-item.p: Williams R et al. ICS peer support framework, 2020.

sec.title: Experience of early postgraduate exposure to intensive care medicine: a phenomenological study
D Enright1, R Colgan2, E O’Connor3
1University of Galway, Galway, Ireland, 2Mater Misericordiae University Hospital, Dublin, Ireland, 3St James’s Hospital, Intensive Care, Dublin, Ireland
sec.p: Critical Care 2023, 27(S1): P354

Introduction: While clinical role transition is known to pose a challenge to doctors in training, and the intensive care unit (ICU) noted to be a demanding workplace environment, relatively little is known about the experience of doctors as they transition to intensive care medicine. Thus, this study aimed to explore the experience of registrar-grade doctors undergoing the transition to intensive care medicine (ICM), and to examine the interplay between personal and professional life during this transition.

Methods: This qualitative study was guided by transcendental phenomenology and multiple and multimodal transitions theory (MMT). Data were collected via eleven semi-structured interviews, which were analysed using a six-step thematic analysis. Peer debriefing, audit trail and a reflexive diary were used.

Results: The experience of doctors undergoing the transition to ICM was found to be influenced by the clinical environment of the ICU, which was noted to be a demanding and potentially stressful workplace, and one in which the support of colleagues was greatly valued (Fig. 1). The individual characteristics of the doctor undergoing the transition impacted upon this transition, as their previous experience and the impact of the transition on their professional development and identity were observed to be influential. In keeping with MMT, the interplay between personal and professional life was significant, as participants outlined the impact of anxiety before a work shift, the emotional and cognitive burden of a shift, and the effect of the transition to ICM on their personal relationships.

Conclusions: This study observes the ICU to be an extremely challenging workplace environment, with a notable influence on the personal lives of those working therein. Nevertheless, ICM offers invaluable opportunity for learning and both personal and professional growth.
Fig. 1 (abstract P354). The key themes (dark blue boxes, white font) and subthemes (light blue boxes, black font) identified

sec.title: A novel approach to collecting feedback about the intensive care setting
L Jackson1, FJ Lamb2, H Leaman2
1Surrey and Sussex Healthcare NHS Trust, Department of Medicine, Redhill, UK, 2Surrey and Sussex Healthcare NHS Trust, Department of Anaesthetics, Redhill, UK
sec.p: Critical Care 2023, 27(S1): P355

Introduction: Obtaining feedback regarding patient care in the intensive care unit (ICU) from family members is challenging. The aim of this study was to investigate if the data collected by Medical Examiners (MEs) could provide insight into relative’s ICU experiences. MEs started in England & Wales in 2020 to enable an independent senior doctor to scrutinise care quality and outcomes for in-hospital deaths [1]. MEs contact Next of Kin (NOK) for feedback about hospital care. At Surrey & Sussex Healthcare NHS Trust (SASH), this narrative feedback is recorded in a bespoke database [2].

Methods: Anonymised ME data from all deceased ICU patients between 1 October 2020 to 31 March 2022 were analysed. Qualitative data analysis was carried out using an inductive thematic approach. Two researchers (LJ & FJL) analysed the data, and a coding framework and themes were identified. A third researcher (HL) checked the analysis. Permission to use the data was gained from SASH.

Results: Analysis included data collected from NOK of 165 deceased ICU patients, including 66 (40%) grown-up children aged > 18 years and 65 (39%) spouses or partner. Of these 102 (50%) were present at moment of death. The four main themes emerged: Communication; Care and treatment; Visiting and Perceptions and understanding (Fig. 1). Across themes, communication was intrinsically linked to all aspects of a NOK’s experience but mostly with patient care. Several clinical learning points were identified including NOK’s involvement in decision making and timing and delivery of complex information.

Conclusions: Using ME collected data has proven a valuable source of insightful feedback. These results can strengthen staff’s knowledge of NOK’s expectations and aid learning to improve patient care.

list-item.p: NHS National Medical Examiners Office. 2020. https://www.england.nhs.uk/establishing-medical-examiner-system-nhs/.
M Mackenzie. Bereavement board. FileMaker Server Installation and database.

caption.p: Fig. 1 (abstract P355). Positive and negative comments grouped by theme

sec.title: The experience of critical care clinicians in breaking bad news to < 18 year-old relatives
A Molokhia1, G Richmond2, A Rochester2, R Breeze2
1Hamad Medical Corporation, Medical Intensive Care, Doha, Qatar, Lewisham and Greenwich NHS Trust, Anaesthetics and Intensive Care, London, UK, 2Lewisham and Greenwich NHS Trust, London, UK
sec.p: Critical Care 2023, 27(S1): P356

Introduction: Given that guidance, literature and training on breaking bad news to < 18-year-old relatives (< 18 YOR) in an adult ICU is very limited, we surveyed critical care (CC) physicians’ current practice and confidence levels surrounding this.
Methods: A Google Form investigating current practice, confidence levels, areas for development and appetite for training was disseminated to CC physicians worldwide.

Results: A total of 194 CC physicians (65% consultants) from 17 countries responded. Of these, 92% reported no training in breaking bad news to < 18YOR and 97% were not aware of any formal guidelines surrounding this. Only 8% felt comfortable breaking bad news to < 18YOR and 97% said that they would be interested in formal training. Roughly 25% of all UK Critical Care (CC) admissions are < 50 years of age [1] and many will have dependents < 18 years old. Despite this, 42% of physicians described varying age restrictions for visiting CC and 7% allow only adults. When breaking bad news to < 18YOR, 79% would seek prior advice from a close adult relative and 41% would like the support of a child psychologist, yet only 11% had access to one.

Conclusions: Our data shows there is a worldwide unmet need for greater guidance and training for physicians when communicating with < 18YOR in CC. We plan to liaise with senior CC physicians and psychology services to develop formal guidelines on breaking bad news to < 18YOR with a view to implementing interactive training sessions to improve clinician confidence.

list-item.p: Secondary Care Analysis. NHS Digital 1–28 2017. https://digital.nhs.uk/data-and-information/publications/statistical/hospital-adult-critical-care-activity/2015-16.

sec.title: Agreement between family members and physician’s view in the ICU environment; the personal experience as a factor in-fluencing the attitude towards corresponding hypothetical situations
P Stamou1, D Tsartsalis2, G Papathanakos1, E Dragioti3, I Andrianopoulos1, M Gouva4, V Koulouras1

1University Hospital of Ioannina, Intensive Care Unit, Ioannina, Greece, 2” Hippokration” Hospital, Department of Emergency Medicine, Athens, Greece, 3University of Ioannina, Laboratory of Psychology of Patients, Families & Health Professionals, School of Health Sciences, Department of Nursing, Ioannina, Greece, 4 University of Ioannina, Laboratory of Psychology of Patients, Families & Health Professionals, School of Health Sciences, Department of Nursing, Ioannina, Greece
sec.p: Critical Care 2023, 27(S1): P357

Introduction: It is not known whether intensive care unit (ICU) patients’ family members realis-tically assess patient's health status. The aim was to investigate agreement between family and intensivists’ assessment concerning patient’s evolution of health, focusing on family members’ resilience and their perceptions about decision-making.

Methods: For each ICU patient, withdrawal criteria were assessed by intensivists while family members assessed the development of patient's health and completed Connor-Davidson Mental Endurance- and Self-Compassion-Scale. Six months after ICU discharge, a follow-up contact was established, and family members responded about two hypothetical scenarios.

Results: 162 ICU patients and 189 family members were recruited. Intensivists decision about whether a patient met the withdrawal criteria had 75.9% accuracy for prediction of survival, while family’s assessments was statistically independent of intensivist’s opinion; resilience had a significant positive effect on probability of agreement with intensivists. Six months after discharge, family members whose relatives were still alive were significantly more likely to consider family or patient himself as those who should be involved in decision-making.

Conclusions: Resilience is related to an enhanced probability of agreement of family with intensivists’ perception of patient’s health evolution. Family attitudes in hypothetical scenarios are significantly biased on the patient's actual health evolution.

sec.title: Communicate: family-oriented communication in the intensive care unit
RH Kochen1, ASY Kraaijkamp1, E Pragt1, PWH Oyen2, EDJM Perree2, MHJ Van den Beuken-van Everdingen2, WNKA Van Mook1
1MUMC, Intensive Care, Maastricht, Netherlands, 2MUMC, Palliative Care Center, Maastricht, Netherlands
sec.p: Critical Care 2023, 27(S1): P358

Introduction: The goal of this prospective observational study was to gain more insight in the current conversation techniques and topics intensivists address during bad news conversations and whether there is room for improvement. The following hypothesis was formed: intensivists can learn from the perspective of palliative care nurses (PCN) with regard to subjects of conversation during end-of-life (EoL) conversations.

Methods: The goal of this prospective observational study was to gain more insight in the current conversation techniques and topics intensivists address during bad news conversations and whether there is room for improvement. The following hypothesis was formed: intensivists can learn from the perspective of palliative care nurses (PCN) with regard to subjects of conversation during end-of-life (EoL) conversations.

Results: The results (n = 11) are shown in Fig. 1. The feedback of the PCN was that the physicians were empathic and genuinely involved and interested in the relatives and patients; they delivered the message professionally and were clear and understanding in their communication. Points of improvement were to more frequently and in more depth clarify the family relationships, to provide more room for the relatives’ emotions, to discuss spirituality broader than from the perspective of religion only and to have the conversations in more suitable rooms. The survey among relatives (n = 9) revealed that the relatives’ valued discussing the medical context, the patient’s goals and wishes, the patient’s and relatives’ emotions and feelings, meaning of life and spirituality.

Conclusions: The PCN’s feedback was valuable and the communication can be improved with regard to the subjects of family relationships, spirituality and providing more room for relatives’ emotions.
Fig. 1 (abstract P358). An overview of actors involved in the EoL conversations and themes resulting from the qualitative analysis. The themes were divided into actors (physician, nurse, relatives and the patient), medical contet and other (arrangements time, ambiance and external influence conversation). The figure shows how the themes are interacted and the most important subjects in the themes

sec.title: Family satisfaction with ICU communication during the COVID-19 pandemic: a prospective multi-centre Australian study
M Reddy1, U Kadam2, JD Lee3, C Chua4, W Wang5, T McPhail6, J Lee7, N Yarwood8, M Majumdar2, A Subramaniam1

1Frankston Hospital, Department of Intensive Care Medicine, Frankston, Australia, 2Werribee Mercy Hospital, Werribee, Department of Intensive Care Medicine, Melbourne, Australia, 3Monash Health Casey Hospital, Berwick, Department of Intensive Care Medicine, Melbourne, Australia, 4Monash University, Clayton, Faculty of Medicine Nursing and Health Sciences, Melbourne, Australia, 5Monash University, Clayton, School of Public Health and Preventive Medicine, Melbourne, Australia, 6Werribee Mercy Hospital, Werribee, Department of Social Work, Melbourne, Australia, 7Monash Health Casey Hospital, Berwick, Department of Social Work, Melbourne, Australia, 8Epworth Hospital Geelong, Waurn Ponds, Department of Intensive Care Medicine, Geelong, Australia
sec.p: Critical Care 2023, 27(S1): P359

Introduction: Virtual communication has become common practice during the COVID-19 pandemic due to visitation restriction. We aimed to evaluate overall family satisfaction in intensive care unit (FS-ICU) with virtual communication strategies during the COVID-19 pandemic period.

Methods: In this prospective multi-centre study involving three metropolitan hospitals in Melbourne, Australia, the next of kin (NOK) of all the eligible ICU patients between 07/01/2022 and 10/31/2020 were required to complete an adopted version of FS-ICU 24-Questionnaire.Group comparisons were analysed for family satisfaction scores: ICU/care (satisfaction with care), FS-ICU/dm (satisfaction with information/decision-making) and FS-ICU total (overall satisfaction with the ICU) were calculated. The essential predictors that influence family satisfaction were identified using quantitative and qualitative analyses.

Results: Seventy-three out of the 227 patients' NOK who initially agreed, completed the FS-ICU questionnaire (response rate 32.2%). The mean (SD) FS-ICU/total was 63.9 (30.8). The mean score for satisfaction with FS-ICU/dm was lower than the FS-ICU/care (62.1 [30.30 vs. 65.4 [31.4]; p 0.001) (Fig. 1). There was no difference in mean FS-ICU/total scores between survivors (n = 65; 89%) and non-survivors (n = 8, 11%). Higher patient APACHE-III score, female NOK and the patient dying in ICU were independent predictors for FS-ICU/total score while a telephone call at least once a day by an ICU doctor was related to higher family satisfaction for FS-ICU/dm.

Conclusions: There was low overall family satisfaction with ICU care and virtual communication strategies adopted during the COVID-19 pandemic. Effort should be targeted for improving factors with virtual communication that cause low family satisfaction during the COVID-19 pandemic.
Fig. 1 (abstract P359). Scores for family satisfaction with care (FS-ICU/care) and decision-making (FS-ICU/dm)

sec.title: Family satisfaction with care in the ICU
RI Ilan1, HK Khalilia2
1Rambam Health Care Campus, Critical Care Medicine, Haifa, Israel, 2Rambam Health Care Campus, School of Medicine, Technion Institute of Technology, Haifa, Israel
sec.p: Critical Care 2023, 27(S1): P360

Introduction: Admission to the ICU is a significant source of stress for patients' families, and may induce anxiety, post-traumatic stress, and depression [1]. We aimed to assess the satisfaction of family members with the quality of care (QOC), communication with clinical staff, and decision-making processes at our ICU. To our knowledge such evaluation has not been performed in Israel before.

Methods: Prospective observational study in the general ICU at the Rambam Health Care Campus in Haifa, Israel. Family members of patients who were admitted for at least 72 h were given a previously validated questionnaire, which included 26 questions concerning QOC, communication and decision-making processes [2].

Results: Of 70 family members that were approached, 45 (64%) responded. Overall satisfaction with QOC was 77% (Table 1). Thirty-eight (84%) of participants were completely or very satisfied with the QOC and communication with ICU staff. No statistically significant differences were found between family members of patients who survived and those who did not. Our findings were similar to those of previously reported studies [3].

Conclusions: In this first of its kind study in Israel, the majority of family members of patients admitted to the ICU expressed a high level of satisfaction with the QOC, communication, and decision-making processes. However, there is place for improvement on various aspects. Further research is required to better understand the reasons for dissatisfaction regarding certain domains, and to then intervene and improve the experience of family members. The results of the present study will allow us to monitor progress over time and test the effectiveness of various interventions to improve the experience of family members in our ICU.

list-item.p: Schaefer KG et al. Curr Opin Crit Care 2009;6:569–77.
Wall RJ et al. Crit Care Med 2007;35:271–279.
Heyland DK et al. Crit Care Med 2002;30:1413–8.

caption.p: Table 1 (abstract P360): Satisfaction with care and decision making process

sec.title: Quantifying caregiver workload during the COVID-19 pandemic using ambient intelligence
N Choudhury1, A Mizrahi1, HA Clark2, T Morris3, L Boyd3, P Chan1
1Eastern Health, Intensive Care Department, Box Hill, Australia, 2Black.AI, Melbourne, Australia, 3Eastern Health, Box Hill, Australia
sec.p: Critical Care 2023, 27(S1): P361

Introduction: The COVID-19 pandemic has increased caregiver workload [1]. It is unclear how this workload is distributed across patients with varying presentations. Ambient intelligence (AmI) utilizes neural networks to monitor multiple data points in video feeds, and automatically tracks various aspects of human movement [2]. AmI was used to examine the workload of healthcare staff in relation to temporal and patient characteristics on a COVID ward at a major metropolitan hospital.

Methods: Sensors were deployed in patient rooms on a COVID ward to detect caregiver visits at 5-min intervals. Electronic medical records were used to identify variables hypothesized to contribute to visits.

Results: 5514 h across 55 patients (mean age 72, range 17–98) were analyzed. The primary reason for admission was medical in 45 cases (81.8%), psychiatric in 8 cases (14.5%) and surgical in 2 cases (3.6%). Medical emergency (MET) calls occurred in 21 (38.2%) cases. As summarized in Fig. 1, visitation was lowest between 0000 and 0400 (27.3 ± 1.1 min/hour (min/hr)) and highest between 1200 and 1600 (65.5 ± 1.3 min/hr). The mean ± SE visitation spent with medical, psychiatric, and surgical patients was 51.7 ± 0.6, 38.8 ± 1.3, and 33.7 ± 3.9 min/hr respectively (p < 0.005). Overall lowest visitation was in surgical patients between 0400 and 0800 (4.5 ± 4.5 min/hr). Mean ± SE visitation were 66.7 ± 4.6 min/hr in the three hours preceding and following MET calls compared to 50.2 ± 0.5 min/hr in periods without MET calls (p < 0.005). There was no difference in visitation time between patients with respiratory symptoms and those without (50.7 ± 0.9 vs 48.8 ± 0.7 min/hr, p = 0.1).

Conclusions: AmI can help quantify patient workload, potentially improving staff planning. Further studies comparing healthcare attendance between patients on COVID wards and non-COVID wards may provide insight into the impact of unique factors associated with the pandemic.

list-item.p: Knobf MT et al. Oncol Nurs Forum. 2022;49:491–499.
Chan PY et al. Aust Crit Care. 2023;36:92–98.

caption.p: Fig. 1 (abstract P361). Caregiver visitation in staffing minutes/hour with respect to time of day (a), and stratified by patient category—medical, psychiatric or surgical (b), whether or not a MET call was activated (c) and whether the patient had COVID-19 related respiratory symptoms (d)

sec.title: Impact of standard versus enhanced personal protective equipment onto team performance during simulated emergency in ICU: randomised controlled trial (PPE-TEAM trial)
M Renza1, RS Sýkora1, DP Peřan2, VZ Zvoníček1, PW Waldauf1, MS Stern1, FD Duška1

1Charles University, Third Faculty of Medicine and FNKV University Hospital, Prague, Czech Republic, Department of Anaesthesia and Intensive Care Medicine, Prague, Czech Republic, 2Emergency Medical Services of the Karlovy Vary Region, Karlovy Vary, Czech Republic, Emergency Medical Services of the Karlovy Vary Region, Karlovy Vary, Czech Republic, Karlovy Vary, Czech Republic
sec.p: Critical Care 2023, 27(S1): P362

Introduction: During the recent COVID-19 pandemics, hospital staff often used personal protective equipment (PPE) beyond the standards recommended by the public health authorities. The aim of the study is to investigate the impact of the additional PPE compared to the standard PPE onto team performance when dealing with a medical emergency.

Methods: Single centre, prospective, randomized, controlled, open-label, study compared 22 teams, consisting of a doctor and 2 nurses, randomized in a 1:1 ratio to use standard PPE or Super-Safe Setup in a simulated medical crisis. Before randomisation, all teams underwent a standardised mock scenario without the use of PPE was used to assess the performance at baseline. The primary outcome was Team Emergency Assessment Measure (TEAM), secondary outcomes included the timing and quality parameters of CPR and physiological changes the rescuers.

Results: As compared to standard PPE recommended by WHO [1], the use of additional PPE that included hooded coverall, significantly reduced non-technical skills of teams (TEAM score 30.6 ± 5.7 vs. 36.2 ± 5.0, p = 0.015) (Table 1), mainly due to reducing performance in complex tasks such as planning of future actions, prioritisation, and following the guidelines. This was also reflected in participants self-assessment score (Table 1). Nonetheless, there was no significant difference in the quality parameters and timing of milestones during a simulated CPR. There were no differences in rescuers physiological responses.

Conclusions: During a simulated crisis, extended PPE compared to the PPE recommended by the World Health Organisation degraded non-technical team skills. This did not translate into measurable deterioration of patient-centred outcomes, such as quality or timing of CPR.

list-item.p: World Health Organization. Technical specifications of personal protective equipment for COVID-19. Interim Guidance.

caption.p: Table 1 (abstract P362). Primary Outcomes—TEAM Score

sec.title: Real-time remote audio-visual system versus standard buddy system to monitor the doffing of personal protective equipment during COVID-19 pandemic: an observational study
BN Naik, MS Lazar, V Ganesh, A Singh, GD Puri
PGIMER, Department of Anaesthesia and Intensive Care, Chandigarh, India
sec.p: Critical Care 2023, 27(S1): P363

Introduction: Literature states a higher self-contamination rate among healthcare workers (HCWs) while doffing personal protective equipment (PPE). During the COVID-19 pandemic, onsite trained observers were not always available to monitor PPE compliance. The remote audio-visual doffing surveillance (RADS) system has the potential to overcome this limitation [1, 2]. We aimed to compare the efficacy of this real-time RADS system against the onsite buddy system for monitoring the doffing of PPE.

Methods: This prospective, observational study was conducted at our tertiary care centre in northern India. Study was registered in the clinical trial registry India (CTRI/2020/11/038172). 200 HCWs who cared for COVID-19 patients in the intensive care units were included. Group A included HCWs who performed doffing with the help of an onsite trained observer and group B included HCWs who performed doffing with the RADS system. An independent observer noted the error at any step using the CDC doffing checklist, in both groups. An online questionnaire to analyse the level of satisfaction post-doffing was also surveyed.

Results: The proportion of errors committed during doffing was significantly lower in group B compared to group A with a low relative risk of 0.34 (95% CI 0.22–0.51) (p < 0.001) (Fig. 1). In both groups, there was no difference in HCWs feedback regarding the ease of the system and fear of committing an error. Though the perceived quality of monitoring was felt better with onsite buddy, the overall confidence rating of being safe after doffing was better with the RADS system.

Conclusions: Real-time RADS system may be more effective than the onsite buddy system for ensuring the safety of HCWs during doffing PPE. HCWs level of satisfaction related to the ease and anxiety with the monitoring systems were comparable. RADS system can reduce reliance on HCW resources and can integrate well into existing healthcare systems.

list-item.p: Singh A et al. Anesth Analg. 2020;131:e112–3.
Naik BN et al. Cureus 2021;13:e18071.

caption.p: Fig. 1 (abstract P363). A Barplot showing the number of participants committing errors in each group; B Point plot showing the predicted margins (incidence rate ratios, with 95% CIs) of committing a greater number of errors/events across occupation designation classes during doffing stratified by experimental groups. (ESW = Environmental Sanitation Workers, RADS = Remote Audio-visual Doffing Surveillance System)

sec.title: Diurnal patterns of noise levels observed in the Whittington Hospital intensive care unit
NP Ivin1, H Montgomery2, S Weerakkody2
1Princess Royal University Hospital, Anaesthetics Department, London, UK, 2University College London, UCL Centre for Human Health and Performance, London, UK
sec.p: Critical Care 2023, 27(S1): P364

Introduction: Delirium and sleep disturbance have deleterious physiological and psychological effects in the critically ill, with associated adverse outcomes including longer length of stay and increased mortality [1]. A contributing factor is the ICU environment; in particular the noise which patients experience. We aimed to describe the characteristics of sound pressure levels (SPLs) that patients and staff are exposed to in an 11 bed bay of a district general hospital in order to target noise limiting interventions.

Methods: SPLs (LAeq 1 min and 1 h; A-weighted continuous equivalent SPL, LAFmax; A-weighted fast constant maximum SPL, and Lpeak; peak SPL) were monitored continuously over a 116 day period using a SoundEar3 (NoiseMeters Ltd) noise activated warning device. Data were extracted and diurnal median and interquartile LAeq values were plotted. Diurnal rates of LAFmax values above different thresholds were plotted. Median numbers of extreme Lpeak values per 24 h period were calculated.

Results: Overall median LAeq was 65.3 dB (interquartile range 62.6–67.5 dB). LAeq 1 min and 1 h plots showed diurnal variation consistent with the timings of key events such as ward rounds and handovers, with daytime LAeq values observed to be lower at the weekend (Fig. 1). Although LAFmax values were much lower at night, at trough levels 5 min per hour had LAFmax > 80 dB. Mean numbers of Lpeak values per 24 h period were 0.67 > 120 dB, 3.15 > 115 dB, 12.0 > 110 dB, and 45.7 > 105 dB.

Conclusions: Our results show a wide diurnal variation in both the ambient and peak noise levels that patients and staff are exposed to, similar to that observed in other centres. Our results show higher noise levels than those measured in comparable centres and higher levels than those that have been recorded in smaller bays, however direct comparisons are difficult due to technical aspects of acoustics.

list-item.p: Salluh JIF et al. BMJ 2015;350:h2538.

caption.p: Fig. 1 (abstract P364): Median and interquartile Laeq (1 min) values by time of day. Arrows and annotations indicate the timing and duration of key events each day, and timing of day and night shifts for medical and nursing teams

sec.title: A systematic review investigating what factors influence clinical decision-making for patients in critical care units regarding the initiation, delivery or titration of organ support
K Matsumoto, H Malcolm, W Thomson, J Prowle, Z Puthucheary, T Stephens
Critical Care and Perioperative Medicine Research Group, William Harvey Research Group, Queen Mary University of London, London, UK
sec.p: Critical Care 2023, 27(S1): P365

Introduction: The critical care unit is a dynamic environment that necessitates daily clinical decision-making. Despite this, the processes and influences on decision-making have been poorly defined. Our aim was to synthesise existing research to identify factors influencing clinicians’ decisions surrounding organ support.

Methods: We conducted a systematic search on three databases (PubMed, Embase and CINAHL) to find relevant papers exploring human factors that influenced decisions made by critical care clinicians. A meta-ethnography was then completed on included papers. The data were collated into a common format and cross-compared. This generated distinct themes/subthemes that were synthesised to develop a higher order interpretation.

Results: 33 studies (from 8647 citations) met the inclusion criteria. 21 of these only included nurses, 7 only doctors and 5 were interprofessional. 11 factors that influenced a clinician’s decision-making were identified: experience; professional and personal risk; uncertainty; characteristics of individuals; senior support; team hierarchy; decision making by colleagues; protocols, guidelines and evidence; time and workload; hospital structure; and clinical condition. These were grouped into four themes: human, team, system and patient factors. From our interpretation of the data, we developed a model of how these themes interact to influence clinicians’ decision-making (Fig. 1).

Conclusions: While decision-making surrounding organ support is complex and dynamic we identified recurring themes that influenced these decisions across different professions and environments. Further research to improve individual decision-making could be structured around addressing these themes.
Fig. 1 (abstract P365). Model for clinical decision making. A clinician interprets a clinical scenario and makes a decision. However there are multiple factors that influence either the clinical scenario or the clinician, and in turn the final decision

sec.title: Critical care unit-to-unit capacity transfers have an increased risk of dying compared to non-transferred patients
F Parenmark1, M Fredrikson2, SM Walther3

1Centre for Research and Development, Uppsala University, Region Gävleborg, Dept. Anaesthesia & Intensive Care, Gävle, Sweden, 2Department of Biomedical and Clinical Sciences, Faculty of Medicine, Linköping University, Linköping, Sweden, 3Department of Cardiothoracic Anaesthesia and Intensive Care, Linköping University Hospital, Linköping, Sweden
sec.p: Critical Care 2023, 27(S1): P366

Introduction: To preserve access to the intensive care unit (ICU)—even in times of a shortage of critical care beds—ICUs adopt temporary measures to make room for new admissions. One measure is to transfer a patient to another ICU; a so-called ‘capacity transfer’. Understanding the impact of capacity transfers on patient outcomes is of utmost importance since these transfers are not carried for the benefit of the patient. The purpose of the current study was to examine the idea that capacity transfer is associated with increased mortality compared to non-transferred patients who either died in the ICU or were discharged alive to a regular hospital ward.

Methods: In this multicentre, case-controlled, matched observational cohort study, we collected data between January 2016 and December 2019 from 68 ICUs. Capacity transferred patients were matched 1:10 using controls from the same ICU, with a stay as long or longer to avoid immortal time bias in the non-exposed population. We compared adjusted 30- and 90-day mortality rates in patients exposed to capacity transfer with non-transferred patients using potential outcomes modelling and mixed effects logistic regression modelling with propensity score adjustment.

Results: 95,909 individuals from 68 ICUs were eligible for inclusion. The main analysis group included 13,670 patients of which 1243 had been transferred due to capacity reasons. Capacity transfer was associated with a risk increase in 30-day mortality of 4.7% (95% CI 2.0–7.4; p = 0.001) compared to non-transferred patients when analysed using a potential outcomes framework (Table 1). The average risk increase in 90-day mortality was 4.9% (95% CI 2.1–7.8: p = 0.001).

Conclusions: This study demonstrates that patients undergoing capacity transfers are exposed to an increased mortality risk with about one additional death for every 20 transferred patients, which was neither caused by illness severity on admission nor by additional factors in the referring ICU.

sec.title: Impact of the pandemic on critical care staff safety climate assessed using safety attitudes questionnaire (SAQ) in all hospitals within the East of England
M Blunt1, P Young1, M Hijjeh2, K Cotton3, M Wright3
1Queen Elizabeth Hospital, Critical Care Centre, King’s Lynn, UK, 2Mid and South Essex NHS FT, Critical Care, Basildon, UK, 3NHE East of England, East of England Critical Care Network, Cambridge, UK
sec.p: Critical Care 2023, 27(S1): P367

Introduction: As part of the last two peer review processes the East of England Critical Care Network have undertaken SAQ [1] involving staff in the 18 hospitals in the region. The questionnaire is divided into 7 scales and allows a wide assessment of safety culture which has been associated with improved outcomes and reduced adverse incidents [2]. The two surveys straddle the Covid-19 pandemic which has had a recognised impact on staff welfare. We postulated that this is likely to be identifiable within the survey and wished to understand the effect on different staff groups and levels of experience.
Methods: The survey was distributed using an online platform and analysed in R.

Results: 1647 questionnaires were completed in 2019 (753) and 2022 (894). Demographic data was collected for role (nursing, medical, AHP, support) and critical care experience (short: < 4 years medium: 4–10 years, long: > 10 years). For all staff Climate scores fell significantly in all scales except stress recognition, most noticeably in job satisfaction and working conditions. This was also the case within the nursing subgroup; however, it was not seen in medical staff (though there was a less pronounced trend). AHPs reported a marked reduction in job satisfaction. Support staff also demonstrated big reductions in all scales including teamworking and safety climate. The fall in job satisfaction and working condition scales were also most profound in staff with 4–10 year’s critical care experience.

Conclusions: The pressures of the pandemic have had a lasting effect on most staff groups, particularly in job satisfaction and working conditions. This appears to have particularly impacted nursing and support staff. Those of moderate experience may have been the staff most directly involved with increased patient care and expectations to undertake managerial roles with reduced training. The impact of this appears to be ongoing despite efforts to support staff well-being.

list-item.p: Sexton J. BMC Health Serv Res 2006;6:44–54.
Colla JB. Qual Saf Health Care 2005;14:364–6.

sec.title: Use of a shirt front mounted video camera (vest-cam) to monitor hand hygiene
E Ben-chetrit1, D Belman2, PD Levin2
1Shaare Zedek Medical Center, Infectious Diseases, Jerusalem, Israel, 2Shaare Zedek Medical Center, Intensive Care Unit, Jerusalem, Israel
sec.p: Critical Care 2023, 27(S1): P368

Introduction: Hand hygiene observations are usually made by an observer during "office hours". Hand hygiene compliance at night/weekends might not be comparable. Using a vest-cam (a camera attached to the shirt front) potentially allows hand hygiene compliance to be monitored at all hours of the day by retrospective analysis of the video footage. In this pilot study we present a comparison of hand hygiene observations made by an observer, versus analysis of vest-cam video taken in parallel.

Methods: Following ethics approval, 20 min observation periods were performed on ICU staff wearing a vest-cam. A human observer recorded hand hygiene opportunities (entrance and exit from the patient areas), and hand hygiene performance. The vest-cam video was analyzed by an independent observer according to the same parameters for hand hygiene opportunities and performance. The two records were then compared.

Results: Twelve observation periods were analyzed. One was excluded due to camera misplacement that resulted in the caregiver's hands not being visible. Hand hygiene performance was identified by the vest cam on 68/81 (84%) occasions versus 58/81 (72%) by the observer (p = 0.09). Mean hand hygiene performance time was 10 ± 8 vs 11 ± 21 s (p = 0.74). Not all hand hygiene performance was associated with defined hand hygiene opportunities. Hand hygiene events were identified on 82 occasions. All events were identified from the vest-cam (82/82, 100%), while (67/82, 82%) were identified by the observer (p < 0.001). Calculated hand hygiene compliance was similar: vest cam vs observer 51/82 (62%) versus 43/67 (64%, p = ns).

Conclusions: Analysis of video footageobtained from a shirt front mounted video camera was able to identify hand hygiene opportunities and performance at least as well as an observer. While this technique may be associated with ethical dilemmas, potentially it allows monitoring of hand hygiene compliance outside usual office hours.

sec.title: Exploring intensive care unit (ICU) consultations with a focus on treatment limitations: a critical incident analysis
ASY Kraaijkamp1, RH Kochen2, BPA Spaetgens3, L Schultinge2, CMH Pinxt2, MHJ Van den Beuken-van Everdingen4, WNKA Van Mook2

1Maastricht University Hospital, Intensive Care Department, Palliative Care Department, Maastricht, Netherlands, 2Maastricht University Hospital, Intensive Care Department, Maastricht, Netherlands, 3Maastricht University Hospital, Geriatric Care department, Maastricht, Netherlands, 4Maastricht University Hospital, Palliative Care Department, Maastricht, Netherlands
sec.p: Critical Care 2023, 27(S1): P369

Introduction: The ICU is frequently consulted when a (frail) patient critically deteriorates on a general ward. However, considering patient-tailored care, this potentially leads to the escalation of care disproportionately to the patient's preferences, pre-existing quality of life, and functioning. Alternatively, other medical specialists, such as the palliative team, can be consulted to discuss less-invasive, comfort-based treatment options. The aim was to gain better insight into the underlying motives behind ICU consultations on general wards with a focus on (prior) documented treatment limitations (such as do-not-resuscitate, do-not-intubate, comfort-only preferences) or for whom intensivists deem ICU treatment too invasive. Concomitantly, to explore the potential role of an earlier involvement of the palliative team in these consultations.
Methods: A qualitative study using a critical incident technique and thematic analysis.

Results: In total, 36 individual interviews were conducted between April and July 2022, with 18 ICU nurses and intensivists(in-training) consulted by general wards of a tertiary academic centre. The palliative team was involved in 6% at the time of consultation, 84% of the patients were estimated to have a life expectancy of fewer than 12 months, and 58% died within 2 months. The themes frequently discussed were awareness of the burdens of ICU treatment, establishing conformity about supervision, and timely communication about treatment limitations. Participants acknowledged the underutilization of the palliative care team due to invisibility (outside office hours) but saw a potentially valuable role in the earlier consultation of this team.

Conclusions: Although intensivists are watchful for inordinate invasive care and share responsibility for counselling or discussing treatment limitations, earlier consultation of potential other actors such as the palliative team might contribute to more patient-tailored care and more efficient use of ICU resources.

sec.title: TEPology: reconsidering treatment escalation planning on ICU
E Harlow, W Woodward
Royal Cornwall Hospital, Critical Care, Cornwall, UK
sec.p: Critical Care 2023, 27(S1): P370

Introduction: Treatment escalation planning (TEP) is a dynamic and nuanced process, but for effective communication and handover is often summarised into a binary decision of whether or not to attempt resuscitation. Do Not Attempt Resuscitation (DNAR) status is recognised in 22 European countries [1] and in the UK, where a TEP proforma is frequently used in patient notes. The UK Guidelines for the Provision of Intensive Care Services (GPICS) states that all patients should have a treatment escalation plan documented [2], but provides little further guidance as to when it should be completed or what form it should take.

Methods: We audited TEP proforma completion on a general ICU in Cornwall, UK, and compared results to patient records that included either a proforma or narrative indices of treatment escalation planning.
Results: 21% of the patient sample had a TEP proforma. 30% had either a TEP proforma or narrative indices of a treatment escalation plan.

Conclusions: Documentation of TEP completion in this patient sample is poor and does not meet GPICS standards. Whilst narrative indices recorded in the Consultant notes were more common than TEP proformas, and likely better represent the complexity of TEP decisions while on ICU, the use of standardised proformas at time of ICU discharge contributes to more effective handover, which is crucial for ongoing care and reduction of readmission rates. Use of the proforma should serve to prompt discussion within the MDT, and with patients and their relatives to inform shared decision making. We plan to make TEP proformas mandatory at time of discharge, and reaudit in 3 months. We also anticipate the soon to be published European Society of Intensive Care Medicine’s Guidelines on End of Life care in ICU for further guidance on this important topic.

list-item.p: The Faculty of Intensive Care Medicine. GPICSv2. https://www.ficm.ac.uk/.
European Resuscitation Council. https://ercguidelines.elsevierresource.com.

sec.title: Hospital mortality after discharge from intensive care – the reality of a tertiary center
JE Mateus, J Rua, AR Nogueira, E Louro, G Almeida, M Simões, P Martins
Centro Hospitalar e Universitário de Coimbra, Intensive Care Unit, Coimbra, Portugal
sec.p: Critical Care 2023, 27(S1): P371

Introduction: The discharge of patients from the intensive care unit (ICU) to a ward is a high risk transition of care [1]. The aim of this study was to identify the incidence and characteristics of patients who died in general wards after discharge from ICU.

Methods: Retrospective study of all patients discharged from a tertiary center ICU from April 2021 to October 2022. Demographic data, severity scores and hospital mortality were evaluated. Statistical analysis was performed by χ2 and t Student tests.

Results: In this period, 715 out of 962 patients were discharged from our ICU and 55 (8%) died in the wards: 33% in the first 5 days, 18% from 6th to 10th day and 49% from 11th day on. Cause of death was: sepsis (47%), irreversible neurological injury (17%), cardiovascular disease (12%) and tracheostomy obstruction (10%). Comparing to the patients who were discharged home alive, the patients who died in the wards were older (average age 66.6 vs 58.0 years), had shorter ICU length of stay (mean 15.5 vs 23.4 days), had a higher percentage of tracheostomies (34.5 vs 14.2%), higher Charlson Comorbidity Index (3.6 vs 2.7), higher APACHE II (23.9 vs 18.9) and SAPS-II (52.8 vs 43.3) at ICU admission, higher SOFA at ICU admission (8.9 vs 7.1) and discharge (4.8 vs 2.9) and higher standardized mortality (53% vs 37%). There were no statistically significant differences concerning cause of ICU admission.

Conclusions: ICU mortality underestimates the true mortality of critically ill patients as the mortality in the first days after ICU discharge can be substantial. Though some deaths are inevitable, other are unexpected and might have been prevented by discharge to high dependence units (lacking in our hospital). In our study, deaths occurred in patients who were more likely to die: older patients with more comorbidities and higher severity scores.

list-item.p: Moreira HE et al. Rev Bras Ter Intensiva 2017;29:63–9.

sec.title: Managing the end-of-life transition in the ICU
K Williams, H Omara, K Russell, M Fathy Elkhonezy, V Metaxa
Kings College Hospital, Intensive Care, London, UK
sec.p: Critical Care 2023, 27(S1): P372

Introduction: Approximately 15–20% of ICU patients will die during their hospital admission, frequently after limitation of treatment. Quality of end-of-life (EoL) care focuses on patient/family support, symptom management, minimising distress, and withdrawing/withholding inappropriate treatment [1]. Following withdrawal of life-sustaining therapy, death can be rapid and potentially distressing, hence a smooth transition is paramount with good communication and adequate symptom control [2].
Methods: Service evaluation of EoL practices in a large, tertiary London hospital. Data collected from 1/5 to 16/6/22 on ICU patients transitioning to EoL care.

Results: 43 EoL patients (Table 1). Treatment limitations were placed for 27 (44%), prior to EoL transition. Treatment withdrawal in 30 (70%) patients: 12 (28%) palliative extubated, 7 (16%) remained intubated but other support withdrawn. 7 (16%) remained on full support. EoL medications were delivered as an infusion in 26 (60%) patients in 24 h before death: 8 (19%) subcutaneously and 18 (42%) intravenously. 8 (19%) remained on high doses with ongoing support, 16 (37%) had reduced infusion rates for comfort only. The most common infused drugs were fentanyl (50–400 mcg/hr), midazolam (20 mg/24 h-10 mg/hr), morphine (15–150 mg/24 h) and glycopyrronium (600–1200 mcg/24 h). Sedation was deep prior to death (RASS-3.8, 24 h and -3.6, 48 h). Reasons for low RASS were severe brain injury, continued full support and agitation. Mean time from EoL transition to death was 1.25 days.

Conclusions: Death in ICU can be challenging, due to the highly technical environment, co-existence of multiple organ failure and inability to communicate with the patient. Despite national guidance, the practicalities of transitioning from curative to EoL care vary, even in a single institution. The more distinct variability was observed around the use of EoL medication, with doses exceeding those used in palliative care settings.

list-item.p: Faculty of Intensive Care Medicine. Care at the end of life, 2019.
Efstathiou N et al. Palliat Med 2020;34:1140–1164.

tr.td: Ref. to external teams: pall care, psychosocial

sec.title: Implementation of the Triggers tool at the critical care unit at the Royal Marsden Hospital
J Gandhi1, N Kapoor1, P Srivastava1, H May-Miller1, A Halley2, J Droney2, P Gruber3, A Tweedle2
1The Royal Marsden Hospital, Palliative/Critical Care, London, UK, 2The Royal Marsden Hospital, Palliative Care, London, UK, 3The Royal Marsden Hospital, Critical Care, London, UK
sec.p: Critical Care 2023, 27(S1): P373

Introduction: Mortality rates for cancer patients admitted to Critical Care Units (CCU) are improving but for certain subgroups remain high. It is recognised that early palliative care input improves symptom control, family support, length of CCU stay and the use of clinical interventions towards the end of life. Triggers is a tool to help identify patients that may benefit from early palliative care intervention [1]. The tool was adapted by the Royal Marsden Hospital to identify emergency admissions onto CCU that may benefit from early palliative care review.

Methods: A local, multi-cycle retrospective review was conducted with data collected through the electronic patient record system. We collected three data sets—one prior to and two (short and long term) following the implementation of the Triggers tool.

Results: Prior to implementation of the tool, data collected between April 2018–April 2019 showed that 149 patients died on CCU or within 30 days of admission. Of these patients, only 56% were referred to the symptom control and palliative care team (SCPCT) however 97% of these patients would have flagged as Triggers positive. Data between February 2020 and January 2021, after implementation of the Triggers tool, showed that 36% of CCU emergency admissions had a Triggers tool completed. Figure 1 shows the results of this sample. 44% of emergency admissions during this time would have flagged as Triggers positive. Overall, 23% of emergency admissions were referred to the SCPCT, of which 46% of these were due to the tool. Data from January to September 2022, showed a Triggers completion rate of 33%. 100% of emergency admissions would have flagged as Triggers positive. Overall, 33% of emergency admissions were referred to SCPCT due to the tool.

Conclusions: The use of this novel bespoke Triggers tool is now a standard part of the admission process to CCU. This complex intervention requires ongoing training and education to ensure continued compliance.

list-item.p: Nadkarni Y et al. Support Care Cancer 2022;30:2173-2181.

sec.title: Are doctors ready to accept Bayesian adaptive trials? The results of a randomized study of intensivists
BE Malley1 , J Levin2 , A Althouse3 , L Bukowski1, J Kahn1, DT Huang1

1University of Pittsburgh, Department of Critical Care Medicine, Pittsburgh, USA, 2University of Pittsburgh, Organizational Behavior Program, Pittsburgh, USA, 3University of Pittsburgh, Division of General Internal Medicine, Pittsburgh, USA
sec.p: Critical Care 2023, 27(S1): P374

Introduction: We evaluated intensivists’ acceptance and understanding of Bayesian adaptive trials and explored the association between acceptance and understanding. Randomized clinical trials (RCTs) are a mainstay of medical research but have multiple drawbacks including cost and inefficiency. Bayesian adaptive trials may address these drawbacks, but are more complex and potentially less accepted by the medical community.

Methods: We surveyed United States intensivists identified from an American Medical Association email list. Participants were randomized to view an abstract describing a hypothetical Bayesian adaptive or traditional frequentist trial. The abstracts were otherwise similar in format and content with results derived from the same simulated data. Participants were then asked about self-perceived and measured understanding as well as acceptance of the trial findings. Survey results were analyzed using proportional odds logistic regression.

Results: There were 273 responses to 592 surveys (46.1%) and the groups were well balanced. The frequentist group had significantly better self-perceived (p < 0.01 and p < 0.01) and measured understanding (p < 0.01, p = 0.08, p < 0.01, and p = 0.04). There was no significant difference in acceptance between groups (p = 0.47 and p = 0.10). Acceptance was significantly correlated with both self-perceived and measured understanding (Table 1). These results did not change substantially when adjusting for age, practice setting, clinical time, and subspecialty. Opinions on the increasing use of Bayesian methods were mixed: 49.5% positive, 39.2% neutral or undecided, and 11.4% negative.

Conclusions: Despite lower understanding of Bayesian adaptive trials compared to frequentist trials among intensivists we found no difference in acceptance of results. Understanding and acceptance were correlated and a high proportion of intensivists were neutral or undecided about Bayesian adaptive trials so our findings suggest further efforts to educate clinicians on these methods are warranted.

sec.title: Community intensive care unit recruitment to improve research efficiency
N Quigley, SM Bagshaw, VI Lau
University of Alberta, Department of Critical Care, Edmonton, Canada
sec.p: Critical Care 2023, 27(S1): P375

Introduction: In Canada, critical care research has traditionally been conducted in academic hospital intensive care units (ICUs). Increasing community ICUs participation (which represents two-thirds of ICU capacity in Canada) in multi-center trials may enhance recruitment, shorten time to completion and improve generalizability and quality of evidence. We aimed to identify community ICU patients that could be screened for and enrolled in large multi-center trials and quantify the potential opportunity associated with greater community ICU participation.

Methods: We first chose 10 landmark critical care trials suitable for community ICUs and extracted data on patient recruitment and study completion. We then retrospectively analyzed all patients admitted to ICU in 2018 in the province of Alberta, Canada and identified those who could have been considered for the chosen landmark trials based on their clinical characteristics. We finally performed a decision tree analysis to describe the number of patients, both in community and academic settings, that could have been eligible for participation in landmark trials. We further estimated the differences in time to completion with inclusion of community ICU patients.

Results: 10,157 adult patients were admitted to Alberta ICUs in 2018. 5650 (55.6%) were in academic centers and 4507 (44.4%) were in community hospitals. The inclusion of community ICU patients in the selected landmark critical care trials would have led on average to a 64% increase in the number of patients potentially enrolled. Furthermore, it would have decreased the mean time to completion of trials from an estimated 2.8 years with academic ICU patients only to 1.6 years (43% reduction in duration).
Conclusions: The inclusion of eligible patients admitted to community ICUs in multi-center clinical trials can accelerate recruitment and reduce time to trial completion.
